items.title: 🚓👮 Guess Who's Afraid of Going to Jail?
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 1962, the great Walter Cronkite began anchoring ‘CBS Evening News’ – a role that helped define him as “the most trusted man in America.” Under Bari Weiss’s leadership, the program and network have since been turned into a soft MAGA mouthpiece.

Good morning! It’s Swin again. In a classic 1994 episode of ‘The Simpsons’ (the TV series that nearly three decades ago “predicted” a disastrous Donald Trump presidency), Homer Simpson seizes on a crime panic and crumbling trust in institutions, and forms a neighborhood watch to patrol Springfield’s streets and enforce vigilante justice. When the local TV newsman asks Homer about the “accusation that your group has been causing more crimes than it’s been preventing,” Homer responds: “I’d be lying if I said my men weren’t committing crimes.”

That was fiction – but it also sums up Donald Trump’s entire second term, thus far. As Trump and his gang break so many laws and commit so many (war) crimes, do they feel invincible? We can answer that for you…

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Team Trump worries that preemptive pardons won’t be enough to get away with all their crimes; 40 Senate Democrats just voted to block arms sales to Israel; and Abdul El-Sayed picks up a key endorsement in Michigan’s Democratic primary for US Senate.

They can read a poll. They won’t admit it publicly, but much of President Donald Trump’s inner circle sees Democrats retaking the House in the 2026 midterms as a foregone conclusion. They also know, at some point in the near future, their liberal enemies will control the White House, again.

And Team Trump takes it for granted that there will be an intense appetite among progressive voters for their politicians to go on their own “ retribution” tour and seek accountability for all of this administration’s

As Zeteo reported in December, the president has already privately discussed preemptive mass federal pardons before this term ends, to gift an extra layer of impunity to his rogues’ gallery of staff and hatchetmen. Last week, the Wall Street Journal followed up on Trump’s “promise” to do this.

Last year, various Trump administration officials made sure to purchase new legal insurance and professional-liability plans, sources familiar with the matter tell me, in anticipation of future investigations or subpoenas from prosecutors and Democrats. (It’s a smart move: Staffers on the House committee investigating Jan. 6 did the same thing before the 2022 elections, anticipating a Republican-run Congress around the corner.)

But in the past few months (including during Trump’s disastrous war in Iran, which has turbo-charged the levels of leaking, backbiting, blame-shifting, and paranoia within Team Trump’s own ranks), I’ve noticed something.

In my conversations with several senior administration officials, as well as other Trump advisers and elite Republicans close to the White House, their anxiety – over what Democrats might do to them after the midterms, or once Trump is out of power – has kicked up a conspicuous notch. Some of them have told me they’ve noticed a growing trend of Democratic politicians making public calls for aggressive prosecutions of Trumplanders in the future – a trend one Trump aide privately lamented as “kind of worrisome.”

And a significant number of senior appointees working in Pete Hegseth’s Pentagon, in Stephen Miller’s White House, and in so many other departments and crime-laboratories of the Trump-Vance administration do not think that federal pardons will be enough.

After all, they say, state and local prosecutors still exist – and though mass pardons from Trump could make their jobs harder, some of those progressive prosecutors have openly signaled to the Trump administration that they will still be there, waiting for those Trump lieutenants and federal agents, once Democrats reconquer the Oval Office.

Even before that happens, a barrage of congressional investigations will likely commence, should Dems on Capitol Hill win back control of the House or Senate this year. Lawsuits can still occur, pardons or no pardons. These are all reasons not only to buy new liability insurance; they’re all good reasons to lawyer up. All of this will very likely cost Trumpworld’s upper crust a lot of money, time, and resources one day soon.

It’s the kind of money they’ve sought to bleed from their own political enemies, as President Trump has spent the past year-plus picking targets for his Justice Department to corruptly harass, selectively investigate, and vindictively prosecute.

Some Trump advisers tell me they’ve recommended to the president and other top Republicans that they should try to get creative in concocting ways to preempt lawsuits, or state and local prosecutions of Trump’s staff – but even in the fevered fantasies of MAGAworld’s legal brain trusts, they know that is a tall order, at best.

“They’re going to try to jail all of us,” a senior Trump administration official vented to me last month, referring to vengeful Democrats.

That may be giving the notoriously squeamish Dem leaders too much credit – BUT: Hungary, where Viktor Orbán once provided a roadmap for Trump’s authoritarian second term, could soon present the model for post-Trump accountability, as its incoming prime minister, Péter Magyar, moves to clean house, probe his predecessor’s corruption, and repair the country’s institutions.

We’ll see what happens there, and here. But for now, there are at least some prominent liberals (and possible 2028 presidential contenders) who’d like Stephen Miller and others to stay scared.

“They’re not always going to be in office,” Illinois Governor JB Pritzker said on MS Now in October. “The people who serve for the president, including all the way down to ICE agents, can be held accountable when there’s a change of administration.”

That day will come. Team Trump knows it.

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s Human Rights Activists News Agency said last week the death toll from US-Israeli strikes stands at more than 3,000, including 1,700 civilians.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,167 people, including at least 172 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 34 people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

More troops: Despite ongoing peace talks with Iran, the US is sending thousands of additional troops into the Middle East in the coming days, according to the Washington Post.

War Powers resolution fails: The Senate yesterday failed to pass a War Powers Resolution that would have prevented Trump from launching further military operations in Iran without congressional approval. None other than Senator John Fetterman joined Republicans to oppose the measure.

Iran’s newest warning: As the US conducts its naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, a military adviser to Iran’s supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, warned that the country may sink US ships, AFP reports.

Last link to southern Lebanon severed: Reuters, citing a senior Lebanese official, reported that Israel has blown up the last remaining bridge linking southern Lebanon with the rest of the country.

‘Intestines riddled with shrapnel’: A Doctors Without Borders medic told Al Jazeera that due to Israeli strikes in Lebanon, children’s intestines have been “riddled with shrapnel,” or their bowels have been “eviscerated, so their intestines were on the outside of their bodies.”

Historic talks? Trump late last night announced that the leaders of Lebanon and Israel would soon speak - a possible phone call that Lebanese officials reportedly said they knew nothing about. Haaretz later reported that an Israeli official said Benjamin Netanyahu and Lebanese President Joseph Aoun would speak later today, though Lebanese officials still haven’t confirmed the possible historic phone call.

A record 40 Democratic senators voted Wednesday to block the sale of bulldozers to Israel, and 36 Dem senators voted to block the sale of 1,000-lb. bombs to Israel, amid its genocide in Gaza, escalating violence in the occupied West Bank, and the disastrous US-Israeli war in Iran.

The joint resolutions of disapproval, offered by Bernie Sanders, failed, but the votes demonstrated the extent to which the politics around Israel have shifted both in the US broadly – 60% of Americans now have a negative view of Israel – and in Congress.

The votes also highlighted a key roadblock to policy changes when it comes to Israel: Chuck Schumer, Democrats’ leader in the Senate, was among the minority of Dems who opposed both measures.

How much money did Texas Democratic Senate candidate James Talarico raise in the first three months of 2026?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

Attacking the Pope: Speaker Mike Johnson is backing Trump’s attacks on Pope Leo XIV. Johnson said yesterday that “if you wade into political waters, you should expect some political response,” before attempting to lecture the pontiff on Christianity.

More money, more problems: The Trump administration has no idea how much money it’ll spend on its illegal war in Iran. White House budget director Russ Vought could not give Congress a rough estimate yesterday of how much emergency funding Trump will want.

Project Powell: Trump once again threatened to fire Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell if he remains in his position after his term expires next month, and vowed to continue the bogus federal investigation into the renovation project at the Fed’s headquarters.

Monopoly takedown: A federal jury found that Live Nation and Ticketmaster are operating as an illegal monopoly over the ticketing industry, violating antitrust law by overcharging people for tickets and decreasing competition. A federal judge will now consider structural changes or even breaking the company up.

Targeting perceived enemies: Trump’s director of national intelligence, Tulsi Gabbard, urged the Justice Department to criminally investigate a whistleblower and former inspector general who helped bring about Trump’s first impeachment trial, after claiming they were part of a “deep state” conspiracy.

Earlier this year, Zeteo reported on the launch of the Peace, Accountability, and Leadership PAC, a pro-Palestinian counterweight to AIPAC, the pro-Israel lobby. On Thursday, PAL PAC made its first Senate endorsement, backing Abdul El-Sayed in Michigan’s competitive Democratic primary.

“Dr. Abdul El-Sayed represents the vibrant heart of the largest Arab American community in the US and families whose lives are directly affected by Israel’s ongoing bombing of Lebanon and genocide in Gaza,” PAL PAC’s executive director Margaret DeReus said in a statement to Zeteo senior politics editor Andrew Perez.

El-Sayed added, “This moment demands moral clarity – leaders who are unafraid to stand up for human rights and international law. I’m proud to have PAL PAC’s support, and I’ll fight for a foreign policy that reflects our values and a government that actually invests in people here at home.”

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Zeteo is aboard the Global Sumud Flotilla, which set sail from Barcelona yesterday with the aim of breaking Israel’s siege of Gaza. With more than 70 boats and some 1,000 individuals expected to join the fleet, it could be one of the biggest civilian-led mobilizations of its kind against Israel’s genocide.

🇸🇩 ‘Breaking point’: Now officially in its fourth year of war, Sudan is at a “breaking point,” the UN warned. Some 34 million people – 65% of the population – are in urgent need of humanitarian support, and are “trapped in a daily struggle to secure food safety,” per the World Food Program.

🌏 Refugee boat capsizes: At least 250 people, including Rohingya refugees and Bangladeshi nationals, are presumed dead or missing after a boat en route to Malaysia capsized in the Andaman Sea, according to the UN. The boat, which departed on April 4, likely sank due to “heavy winds, rough seas and overcrowding.”

🇹🇷 2 school shootings in 2 days: At least nine people, including children, are dead after an eighth-grade student opened fire at a middle school in south-central Turkey yesterday. The news came just a day after a former student opened fire in a high school in southeast Turkey, wounding 16 people. Both assailants are now dead.

The New York Post just revealed that our Health and Human Services Secretary, RFK Jr., is an “avid animal genitalia researcher.” This man is truly a freak.

Zeteo’s Melanie Riehl and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 1962, the great Walter Cronkite began anchoring ‘CBS Evening News’ – a role that helped define him as “the most trusted man in America.” Under Bari Weiss’s leadership, the program and network have since been turned into a soft MAGA mouthpiece.

Good morning! It’s Swin again. In a classic 1994 episode of ‘The Simpsons’ (the TV series that nearly three decades ago “predicted” a disastrous Donald Trump presidency), Homer Simpson seizes on a crime panic and crumbling trust in institutions, and forms a neighborhood watch to patrol Springfield’s streets and enforce vigilante justice. When the local TV newsman asks Homer about the “accusation that your group has been causing more crimes than it’s been preventing,” Homer responds: “I’d be lying if I said my men weren’t committing crimes.”

That was fiction – but it also sums up Donald Trump’s entire second term, thus far. As Trump and his gang break so many laws and commit so many (war) crimes, do they feel invincible? We can answer that for you…

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Team Trump worries that preemptive pardons won’t be enough to get away with all their crimes; 40 Senate Democrats just voted to block arms sales to Israel; and Abdul El-Sayed picks up a key endorsement in Michigan’s Democratic primary for US Senate.

They can read a poll. They won’t admit it publicly, but much of President Donald Trump’s inner circle sees Democrats retaking the House in the 2026 midterms as a foregone conclusion. They also know, at some point in the near future, their liberal enemies will control the White House, again.

And Team Trump takes it for granted that there will be an intense appetite among progressive voters for their politicians to go on their own “ retribution” tour and seek accountability for all of this administration’s

As Zeteo reported in December, the president has already privately discussed preemptive mass federal pardons before this term ends, to gift an extra layer of impunity to his rogues’ gallery of staff and hatchetmen. Last week, the Wall Street Journal followed up on Trump’s “promise” to do this.

Last year, various Trump administration officials made sure to purchase new legal insurance and professional-liability plans, sources familiar with the matter tell me, in anticipation of future investigations or subpoenas from prosecutors and Democrats. (It’s a smart move: Staffers on the House committee investigating Jan. 6 did the same thing before the 2022 elections, anticipating a Republican-run Congress around the corner.)

But in the past few months (including during Trump’s disastrous war in Iran, which has turbo-charged the levels of leaking, backbiting, blame-shifting, and paranoia within Team Trump’s own ranks), I’ve noticed something.

In my conversations with several senior administration officials, as well as other Trump advisers and elite Republicans close to the White House, their anxiety – over what Democrats might do to them after the midterms, or once Trump is out of power – has kicked up a conspicuous notch. Some of them have told me they’ve noticed a growing trend of Democratic politicians making public calls for aggressive prosecutions of Trumplanders in the future – a trend one Trump aide privately lamented as “kind of worrisome.”

And a significant number of senior appointees working in Pete Hegseth’s Pentagon, in Stephen Miller’s White House, and in so many other departments and crime-laboratories of the Trump-Vance administration do not think that federal pardons will be enough.

After all, they say, state and local prosecutors still exist – and though mass pardons from Trump could make their jobs harder, some of those progressive prosecutors have openly signaled to the Trump administration that they will still be there, waiting for those Trump lieutenants and federal agents, once Democrats reconquer the Oval Office.

Even before that happens, a barrage of congressional investigations will likely commence, should Dems on Capitol Hill win back control of the House or Senate this year. Lawsuits can still occur, pardons or no pardons. These are all reasons not only to buy new liability insurance; they’re all good reasons to lawyer up. All of this will very likely cost Trumpworld’s upper crust a lot of money, time, and resources one day soon.

It’s the kind of money they’ve sought to bleed from their own political enemies, as President Trump has spent the past year-plus picking targets for his Justice Department to corruptly harass, selectively investigate, and vindictively prosecute.

Some Trump advisers tell me they’ve recommended to the president and other top Republicans that they should try to get creative in concocting ways to preempt lawsuits, or state and local prosecutions of Trump’s staff – but even in the fevered fantasies of MAGAworld’s legal brain trusts, they know that is a tall order, at best.

“They’re going to try to jail all of us,” a senior Trump administration official vented to me last month, referring to vengeful Democrats.

That may be giving the notoriously squeamish Dem leaders too much credit – BUT: Hungary, where Viktor Orbán once provided a roadmap for Trump’s authoritarian second term, could soon present the model for post-Trump accountability, as its incoming prime minister, Péter Magyar, moves to clean house, probe his predecessor’s corruption, and repair the country’s institutions.

We’ll see what happens there, and here. But for now, there are at least some prominent liberals (and possible 2028 presidential contenders) who’d like Stephen Miller and others to stay scared.

“They’re not always going to be in office,” Illinois Governor JB Pritzker said on MS Now in October. “The people who serve for the president, including all the way down to ICE agents, can be held accountable when there’s a change of administration.”

That day will come. Team Trump knows it.

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s Human Rights Activists News Agency said last week the death toll from US-Israeli strikes stands at more than 3,000, including 1,700 civilians.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,167 people, including at least 172 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 34 people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

More troops: Despite ongoing peace talks with Iran, the US is sending thousands of additional troops into the Middle East in the coming days, according to the Washington Post.

War Powers resolution fails: The Senate yesterday failed to pass a War Powers Resolution that would have prevented Trump from launching further military operations in Iran without congressional approval. None other than Senator John Fetterman joined Republicans to oppose the measure.

Iran’s newest warning: As the US conducts its naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, a military adviser to Iran’s supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, warned that the country may sink US ships, AFP reports.

Last link to southern Lebanon severed: Reuters, citing a senior Lebanese official, reported that Israel has blown up the last remaining bridge linking southern Lebanon with the rest of the country.

‘Intestines riddled with shrapnel’: A Doctors Without Borders medic told Al Jazeera that due to Israeli strikes in Lebanon, children’s intestines have been “riddled with shrapnel,” or their bowels have been “eviscerated, so their intestines were on the outside of their bodies.”

Historic talks? Trump late last night announced that the leaders of Lebanon and Israel would soon speak - a possible phone call that Lebanese officials reportedly said they knew nothing about. Haaretz later reported that an Israeli official said Benjamin Netanyahu and Lebanese President Joseph Aoun would speak later today, though Lebanese officials still haven’t confirmed the possible historic phone call.

A record 40 Democratic senators voted Wednesday to block the sale of bulldozers to Israel, and 36 Dem senators voted to block the sale of 1,000-lb. bombs to Israel, amid its genocide in Gaza, escalating violence in the occupied West Bank, and the disastrous US-Israeli war in Iran.

The joint resolutions of disapproval, offered by Bernie Sanders, failed, but the votes demonstrated the extent to which the politics around Israel have shifted both in the US broadly – 60% of Americans now have a negative view of Israel – and in Congress.

The votes also highlighted a key roadblock to policy changes when it comes to Israel: Chuck Schumer, Democrats’ leader in the Senate, was among the minority of Dems who opposed both measures.

How much money did Texas Democratic Senate candidate James Talarico raise in the first three months of 2026?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

Attacking the Pope: Speaker Mike Johnson is backing Trump’s attacks on Pope Leo XIV. Johnson said yesterday that “if you wade into political waters, you should expect some political response,” before attempting to lecture the pontiff on Christianity.

More money, more problems: The Trump administration has no idea how much money it’ll spend on its illegal war in Iran. White House budget director Russ Vought could not give Congress a rough estimate yesterday of how much emergency funding Trump will want.

Project Powell: Trump once again threatened to fire Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell if he remains in his position after his term expires next month, and vowed to continue the bogus federal investigation into the renovation project at the Fed’s headquarters.

Monopoly takedown: A federal jury found that Live Nation and Ticketmaster are operating as an illegal monopoly over the ticketing industry, violating antitrust law by overcharging people for tickets and decreasing competition. A federal judge will now consider structural changes or even breaking the company up.

Targeting perceived enemies: Trump’s director of national intelligence, Tulsi Gabbard, urged the Justice Department to criminally investigate a whistleblower and former inspector general who helped bring about Trump’s first impeachment trial, after claiming they were part of a “deep state” conspiracy.

Earlier this year, Zeteo reported on the launch of the Peace, Accountability, and Leadership PAC, a pro-Palestinian counterweight to AIPAC, the pro-Israel lobby. On Thursday, PAL PAC made its first Senate endorsement, backing Abdul El-Sayed in Michigan’s competitive Democratic primary.

“Dr. Abdul El-Sayed represents the vibrant heart of the largest Arab American community in the US and families whose lives are directly affected by Israel’s ongoing bombing of Lebanon and genocide in Gaza,” PAL PAC’s executive director Margaret DeReus said in a statement to Zeteo senior politics editor Andrew Perez.

El-Sayed added, “This moment demands moral clarity – leaders who are unafraid to stand up for human rights and international law. I’m proud to have PAL PAC’s support, and I’ll fight for a foreign policy that reflects our values and a government that actually invests in people here at home.”

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Zeteo is aboard the Global Sumud Flotilla, which set sail from Barcelona yesterday with the aim of breaking Israel’s siege of Gaza. With more than 70 boats and some 1,000 individuals expected to join the fleet, it could be one of the biggest civilian-led mobilizations of its kind against Israel’s genocide.

🇸🇩 ‘Breaking point’: Now officially in its fourth year of war, Sudan is at a “breaking point,” the UN warned. Some 34 million people – 65% of the population – are in urgent need of humanitarian support, and are “trapped in a daily struggle to secure food safety,” per the World Food Program.

🌏 Refugee boat capsizes: At least 250 people, including Rohingya refugees and Bangladeshi nationals, are presumed dead or missing after a boat en route to Malaysia capsized in the Andaman Sea, according to the UN. The boat, which departed on April 4, likely sank due to “heavy winds, rough seas and overcrowding.”

🇹🇷 2 school shootings in 2 days: At least nine people, including children, are dead after an eighth-grade student opened fire at a middle school in south-central Turkey yesterday. The news came just a day after a former student opened fire in a high school in southeast Turkey, wounding 16 people. Both assailants are now dead.

The New York Post just revealed that our Health and Human Services Secretary, RFK Jr., is an “avid animal genitalia researcher.” This man is truly a freak.

Zeteo’s Melanie Riehl and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

items.title: Former MAGA Influencer Admits: It’s All for Money
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex. In tonight’s edition, we take an inside look at how the MAGA grifting class truly operates – and what they really worship, a scandal in the world of media newsletters, and the violent racism directed towards a progressive congressional candidate because he had the audacity to speak another language.

For one brief moment this week, it seemed that Donald Trump’s deranged behavior had finally reached a point where even his most diehard loyalists in the MAGA media ecosystem couldn’t stand behind him.

That blowback, of course, was short-lived and quickly replaced by praise for the president. And with the conservative attention economy showing the need for pro-Trump grifters to stay in their MAGA lane to keep the dollars flowing, we have someone who was deep inside that bubble spilling the tea on how this exactly works.

Seemingly spurred by a ‘60 Minutes’ segment that focused on Pope Leo’s criticism of the Iran war, Trump raged against the pontiff online, culminating in his posting an AI-generated meme depicting himself as Jesus Christ. That did not go over well with MAGA’s Christian influencer class.

“It’s just a sacrilegious post. To put oneself in the position of Christ is just sacrilegious,” Daily Wire host Michael Knowles declared on Monday. “God shall not be mocked,” fifth-place collegiate swimmer turned anti-trans podcaster Riley Gaines tweeted.

Others followed suit, such as right-wing Christian influencers Allie Beth Stuckey and Megan Basham, who both labeled the image as “blasphemous” while urging him to take the post down and ask for forgiveness.

But criticizing Trump – even if it’s deserved – isn’t good for business in the long term for the MAGA grifters, ragebait hustlers, and professional posters that make up the right-wing infotainment complex.

Thankfully, Trump provided them an offramp, ridiculously claiming the image wasn’t depicting him as Jesus, but as a doctor.

Speaking to CBS News afterwards, Trump said he took down the image because he “didn’t want to have anybody be confused,” insisting once again that it was supposed to be him as a doctor. He also took a shot at Gaines, a sycophantic booster of his, when asked if her criticism led to him deleting the post.

“I didn’t listen to Riley Gaines. I’m not a big fan of Riley, actually,” he said, seemingly ignoring his past praise of her.

And while Gaines did what countless other Trump lackeys have done in the past, which is pledge her “love” for the president despite being personally humiliated by him, it is the response of the rest of the right-wing media crowd that really tells the tale.

Trump’s ridiculous explanation is all it took. Suddenly, there appeared to be a coordinated effort by MAGA media figures – many of whom had been loudly critical of the Jesus image – to take the president at his word and amplify the message that Trump wasn’t sacrilegiously depicting himself as the son of god.

Knowles said it was “right and responsible” for Trump to delete the post, though he “doubted” that the president had personally posted the meme in the first place (it should be noted that Trump did admit to posting the image). “I do believe that he didn’t think of this as a depiction of Jesus when posting,” Stuckey tweeted.

Two MAGA personalities – podcaster Graham Allen and Newsmax host David Harris Jr. – posted nearly identical screeds defending Trump: “I don’t believe the president intended to depict himself as Jesus when he posted it,” both tweets proclaimed. “He is a believer after all.”

Candace Owens – who was recently excommunicated from MAGA by the president – accused the pair of “political protestation,” as their “ideas are whatever the highest bidder wants their ideas to be.” Harris admitted he’d sent Allen’s tweet to his team to post something similar, but denied getting paid to post. Allen also said it was false to suggest he was paid for his tweet.

This is not the first time that we’ve seen the right-wing grifter community get in lockstep on a particular narrative and amplify it across social media.

And Ashley St. Clair, a former Turning Point USA brand ambassador and Babylon Bee staffer who was so ingrained in the conservative influencer game that she had a child with Elon Musk, recently pulled the curtain back on the vast underground network behind pro-Trump influence campaigns.

“MAGA and really MAGA, not so much just GOP messaging, is incredibly coordinated,” St. Clair said in a TikTok video last week. “Their main avenues for this influence are group chats, paid influence campaigns and bloggers.”

“Now through the group chats, they operate with large influencers – particularly on Twitter, that is their primary mode of communication, is Twitter – and they will have these giant group chats, that make influencers and individuals feel important, because they are on group chats with members of the administration, people on the Trump team, in which they coordinate these messaging tactics,” she continued.

Further, laying out the pay-to-play apparatus run by GOP consulting firms and the administration, St. Clair explained that there are even platforms built by MAGA operatives where influencers can opt in to specific campaigns to promote specific talking points and narratives.

Noting that compensation is either based on a flat fee or per click, St. Clair compared it to an advertising brand deal. The only difference, she added, is that there is no disclosure requirement. On top of that, she revealed that she was among the influencers who were offered cash to promote Ric Grenell’s push for secretary of state in late 2024. (Politico reported at the time that there was “no evidence” Grenell was directly involved with the campaign, and he declared that the story was not true.)

Still, we’ve seen a growing number of prominent right-wing media personalities – namely Owens, Megyn Kelly, and Tucker Carlson – distance themselves from Trump and his administration over the Iran war, Israel, the economy, and the president’s increasingly erratic behavior.

In a separate video, St. Clair explained that this was also a calculated move to get in front of the sinking Trump ship, noting that we are “going to see a lot more” of this from so-called “independent” political influencers.

“They are going to kind of rebrand in a Megyn Kelly kind of way – ‘this time Trump is taking it too far’ – instead of actually admitting that they were wrong, and that Trump has been this way the whole time, and that they were not able to see it,” she concluded.

Whether they are reinventing themselves now or two years down the line, one thing is clear – everyone in the right-wing industrial outrage complex will need to eventually hitch their wagons to a new meal ticket if they want to keep the cash flowing.

This was a long time coming.

Ever since Mediaite founder Dan Abrams announced in late January that the media news site was launching One Sheet, a newsletter that would charge readers $8 a month to read summaries of other subscription-based newsletters, there had been a growing speculation among media reporters and writers that the author – Mediaite founding editor Colby Hall – had been leaning on AI to produce One Sheet.

[Disclaimer: I was previously a writer and editor for Mediaite, and Hall was the site’s top editor during part of my tenure there.]

Those suspicions were largely validated this week when Hall was suspended after fabricating a quote in Monday’s edition, which resulted in Status’s Oliver Darcy and Semafor’s Max Tani reporting on the slew of mishaps and errors that had plagued the newsletter since its inception.

Writing that he “had noticed many of these glaring errors” recently, Darcy said it didn’t warrant a story until Hall falsely claimed that Status “walked back” its report on CNN’s succession plan – all while making up a quote and attributing it to Status editor Jon Passantino.

Hall admitted to Semafor that he used AI “in a limited way as part of my editorial workflow,” though he asserted that all “written ideas, angles, summaries, takes, and editorial judgments are mine.” After claiming in two bizarre editor’s notes that he merely misattributed the Status quote, he then claimed to Semafor that it came from entering his “own misinterpretation into my sourcing notes,” owing this and other mistakes to data entry issues.

After Darcy presented Mediaite more than half a dozen instances in which Hall misattributed reporting or outright made up quotes and stories, Joe DePaolo – the site’s editor in chief – revealed that Hall had been sidelined pending an investigation.

“We presented your findings to Colby Hall who insists the errors were purely a result of sloppiness in how he aggregated and categorized information, not from the use of A.I.,” DePaolo said in a statement. “Regardless, it is completely unacceptable and Colby has been suspended from Mediaite pending further investigation.”

Meanwhile, Mediaite remains committed – for now – to One Sheet, stating that the product “will continue” and that “someone else will be writing it.” (While Wednesday’s edition was tagged with a staff byline, Zeteo has learned that Lindsey Ellefson – who had served as the newsletter’s producer – will author it going forward.)

“This newsletter throws some punches, (and to be clear, it won’t stop doing that), but it has to be able to take punches too. This one was deserved,” One Sheet noted in Wednesday’s newsletter. “A tip of the hat to Darcy for his work on this, and an apology to him and the others who had words attributed to them in this space that they did not say.”

Reached for comment, Hall told Zeteo that he would let his remarks to Tani speak for themself for now.

Jay Vaingankar, a progressive Gen Z South Asian candidate running for Congress in New Jersey’s 12th District, has found himself on the receiving end of racist attacks and death threats after he posted a campaign video in Hindi earlier this month.

The son of Hindi-speaking immigrants, Vaingankar – one of 13 Democrats vying for the seat – released the ad to reach voters in the demographically diverse district. Data show that two in five households in the area speak a primary language other than English, with Hindi among the most common.

It didn’t take long for anti-immigrant and outright bigoted far-right influencers, such as the anonymous End Wokeness account and “proud Islamophobe” Laura Loomer, to blast the ad – calling it “America Last.”

While NJ.com already reported on the social media hate blitz aimed at Vaingaker, the 28-year-old’s campaign shared with Zeteo that it had also received extremely concerning and profane emails and direct messages – including one threatening to report the US-born candidate to ICE in order to stop his campaign.

“You are despicable & you will be deported. I will call ICE and I will make sure you lose this election. I hate you and everyone like you,” one person, in an email with the subject line “Speak English!,” exclaimed.

Additionally, replies to some of the right-wing influencers’ social media attacks included overt threats. “They’re doing it again because you didn’t hang them last time,” one commenter posted, while another said: “The only answer is blood spilled. Sucks, but that’s the truth.”

Though campaign manager Devontae Freeland lamented that the local New Jersey political establishment has “mostly been silent” on the wave of anti-immigrant hate directed at Vaingankar, he noted that prominent Democrats across the country have expressed solidarity with the campaign. And that has also resulted in endorsements.

“I am proud to endorse Jay Vaingankar for Congress, and I look forward to his leadership in our nation’s capital,” Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi (D-IL) announced this week.

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

items.title: Zeteo Joins the Gaza Flotilla as It Sets Sail to Break Israel’s Blockade
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
A note from our Editor-in-Chief:

I’m happy to share that Zeteo is aboard the Global Sumud Flotilla, which just set sail from Spain with the aim of reaching Gaza. Freelance journalist Alex Colston will be filing dispatches, like his first one below, videos, and updates from the sea for Zeteo, as dozens of boats and what’s expected to be more than 1,000 participants attempt to break Israel’s blockade of the Strip. This work is incredibly important, but it’s not easy. Your support helps us be able to commit to projects like these.

If you’re not already a paid Zeteo subscriber, be sure to become one today to ensure you get all of Alex’s updates – in full, without a paywall! – from the flotilla over the next few weeks. And if you already are one, you can always support our work – and projects like these – further with a donation.

-Mehdi

I’m writing from the waters off the coast of Barcelona, not long after setting sail today aboard the Iqrit, a 50-foot schooner named after a Palestinian village in upper Galilee near Lebanon that was depopulated during the 1948 Nakba. I am sailing with the Global Sumud Flotilla (GSF), a maritime direct action organization committed to breaking Israel’s siege and blockade by sea and by land, alongside hundreds of international activists, humanitarian workers, doctors and clinicians, teachers, journalists, and members of Greenpeace and Open Arms. With thousands of participants from across the world, comprising over 50 national delegations, GSF has been preparing for eight months to sail this spring.

The goal is technically a relatively simple one: sail some boats – carrying food, medical supplies, baby formula, and other aid – from any available port in the Mediterranean to the shores of Gaza in Palestine. But since 2010, these attempts have been anything but easy.

In fact, they’ve been deadly and routinely dangerous. Due to the Israeli blockade, in place on land and at sea, no ships have been allowed to reach Gaza. Aboard the mission last fall, I witnessed first-hand failing to reach Gaza’s shores, despite sailing with a proper flotilla of over 40 ships, with 500 people and brief naval escorts from Spain, Italy, and Turkey. Participants are hoping that, with a much larger fleet, the outcome will be different this year. The current convoy, which partly set sail from Barcelona with 38 boats and nearly 300 participants, is expected to more than double in size compared to the last effort, with potentially 120 ships and thousands of sailors, and the voyage is slated to last about a month. More ships will join over that time. The convoy now also includes support from Greenpeace’s Arctic Sunrise vessel and a tugboat from Open Arms, a maritime organization that conducts search-and-rescue operations for migrants and refugees in the Mediterranean.

Previous missions, including the last one, have been violently intercepted by Israeli forces. Israel has bombed the ships at port and at sea. It’s rammed, deployed water cannons, and used live ammunition against the ships with naval vessels and helicopters. It’s jammed communication devices, sent divers to destroy the ships’ mechanical systems, and planted incriminating evidence aboard the ships. It’s dropped chemical devices from drones. Israeli forces have boarded the ships at gunpoint. They’ve detained and quarantined those aboard the ships, including me, in international waters, which is a violation of international law. When we are arrested, we are brutalized, placed in isolating confinement, deprived of basic medical needs, and they have even sexually violated the flotilla’s prisoners. At every imaginable cost, whether it’s a ragtag group of international civilians or officials from a major country, Israel does not want anyone to accomplish what should be a mundane and unheroic effort: sail to Gaza in solidarity with the Palestinian people and support them in the time of their fully publicized genocide.

With the region engulfed in war, the genocide in Gaza has fallen out of view, though conditions remain dire with starvation and ongoing bombardments. On Tuesday, 11 Palestinians, including a 3-year-old and a 14-year-old, were killed by Israeli attacks across the Gaza Strip, in the latest Israeli violation of the so-called “ceasefire” with Hamas. The Palestinian-led efforts to reconstruct Gaza continue to be stalled by expanding Israeli belligerence and wider indifference to the conditions in Gaza, with Israeli control of border crossings severely limiting international aid efforts, and with war overtaking international attention.

For GSF, these are reasons enough to sail, but the stakes have been raised by the regional war theater. GSF spokespeople have not been swayed by internal deliberations and external conditions to stop or delay sailing. “This is not a risk-free mission,” Brazilian activist Thiago Ávila, one of the GSF’s lead organizers, told Zeteo. “Nonetheless, a nonviolent solidarity mission is a fraction of the risk that Palestinians have gone through every single day for eight decades.”

Following an investigation opened last October for alleged torture and mistreatment of the flotilla participants by Israeli prison guards, Italy’s Office of The Public Prosecutor in Rome is preparing a “letter rogatory,” or request for judicial assistance, to send to Israel, according to the Andalou News Agency. While detained, participants were given an informal hearing with an Israeli judge. The group of prisoners I was with was led to a makeshift shed in the Negev desert to talk with the judge, who asked about the prison conditions. When we told him about the abusive conditions, he told us, “I will tell them these things, but I don’t know if they will listen to me.”

The ship I’m sailing (registered as Mystere) has a mainsail painted with the map of unoccupied and liberated Palestine. The ship’s cabin is plain and necessarily hardscrabble for living on the sea – the wood-paneled engine rises up in the middle to use as a table, about two feet respectively from the navigation desk and compact kitchen on either side – with just enough berths for the six people on board, if you include the settees and dinettes (benches, basically).

This time around, the flotilla’s participants are not limited to captains, crew, boat organizers, journalists, celebrity activists, and official political representatives. There are eco-builders, health and psychological professionals, and educators who have joined on the assumption that reaching and aiding Gaza remains possible, while GSF is sailing to support the Palestinian-led effort of reconstruction in the besieged enclave and to end the US and Israel’s wars of aggression and expansion against Iran and Lebanon. Those new geopolitical conditions present higher risks for this mission compared with the previous efforts.

“The Israeli apartheid colonial system in Palestine and US imperialism, now led by Donald Trump, has acted with total impunity. Their masks are off, and they have shown a lot of willingness to do harm and kill civilians, so that’s why we taking all the measures we can on our mission to adapt to these specific circumstances,” Ávila said, while he and other Global Sumud Flotilla (GSF) steering committee members and organizers were cramped in the cockpit of a modest sailboat during a longform interview a day before launch. Organizers say they will be measuring risks against the impact of mobilizing support for the mission, and if the risks outweigh the impact, they will reassess their strategy.

A GSF press statement shared today stated that there will be a mid-journey stop at ports in Italy, where GSF’s fleet will combine with both an Italian fleet and the 20 ships that set sail with The Thousand Madleens from Marseille on April 4. The flotilla is building “toward its consolidated departure from Siracusa on April 24,” the statement said. Land mobilizations in solidarity with the sea mission are also planned across Europe and the US, including potential strike actions by trade unions in Italy’s ports to ensure the mission’s safe passage to Gaza.

The general public sees these flotillas mostly in social media and news reports: every few months, some ships appear on the Mediterranean with prominent public figures aboard, and they announce they are sailing to break Israel’s siege in Gaza. What’s often not known is the ships sailing to Gaza began in 2008 and immediately succeeded that same year, after the siege conditions escalated in 2007. The initial five efforts of the “Free Gaza Movement” reached the Strip by way of two small fishing boats; these were the first times people had reached the enclave by sea independent of Israeli control since the occupation began in 1967.

Greta Berlin, one of the five organizers of these early efforts, recently recounted to GSF’s US delegation that it was “the most joyous experience” of her life when they arrived by boat, with Palestinians swimming to the boat and then sailing off the coast with their visitors.

Later, in 2010, a large vessel named the Mavi Marmara, which sailed from Turkey, was violently intercepted by Israeli forces, who killed nine participants with live ammunition fired from a helicopter. The Israeli soldiers responsible for the attack never faced any consequences for the killings, and family members of those killed and some participants from that mission still return to support the current efforts. For the next 15 years, however, organizers would continue to try and revive this sailing tactic but to little success – with their attempts defeated by bureaucratic means, bombings, and ramming, or light interceptions – and with minor public fanfare.

It was in 2025 when the Madleen in June, the Handala in July, and the latest Sumud flotilla in September captured wider public attention through publicizing Israeli interceptions and attacks – Israel attacked 13 boats with 16 total deployed projectiles, including chemical and incendiary devices – as well as Israel’s abusive prison conditions. Behind the scenes, the various organizations that had either sailed with the Freedom Flotilla Coalition or Thousand Madleens, marched across North Africa with the Maghreb Sumud Convoy, or provided aid to Gaza through the Sumud Nusuntara have been joining forces through GSF’s flotilla missions.

GSF is composed of a central steering committee, an international general assembly of delegations, a coordination team, and working groups that together boast representation and participation from over 50 national delegations. As steering committee members repeatedly told captains, crew, and boat organizers in meetings last week, GSF aims to be a “revolutionary international model for global justice.” This stated model is sometimes horizontal in ethics and at other times purely vertical in political decision-making, creating a tension that intensifies under the conditions of sailing, political action, and state violence.

The price tag for Gaza’s reconstruction has been estimated to be upwards of $70 billion, a costly redevelopment project that Trump claimed he would personally oversee through his so-called “Board of Peace,” slated to manage reconstruction in coordination with a Palestinian technocratic committee. According to GSF, international inaction and war has prevailed in its place, taking public attention away from Palestinians in Gaza who still suffer under genocidal conditions and occupation.

Now, GSF says, the ships are not only sailing to break the siege, because the scope of its intervention has changed with the changing conditions: They are sailing against the US and Israel’s war on Iran and Lebanon, to open a humanitarian sea corridor to help with Palestinian-led reconstruction efforts, to put pressure on the international community with parallel actions on land, and to demand that states act to end these wars of impunity and the genocide in Palestine.

Alex Colston is an independent journalist, editor, educator, and political organizer. He lives in Brooklyn, New York, but he prefers to be at sea.

Check out more from Zeteo:

Freelance journalist Alex Colston will be filing dispatches, like his first one below, videos, and updates from the sea for Zeteo, as…

items.title: EXCLUSIVE POLL: Michigan’s Dem Senate Race Is Wide Open, AIPAC Is Toxic, Hasan Piker Is Not
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
🚨 HUGE NEWS! Our podcast, ‘We’re Not Kidding’ is nominated for a Webby’s People’s Voice Award, and we need your help to win! Vote for us before Thursday, April 16.

Good morning! Andrew here to break down the results of a poll of the Michigan Democratic Senate primary conducted exclusively for Zeteo and Drop Site News. What’s the upshot? Well, for one thing, voters care a lot less about Hasan Piker’s political influence than that of AIPAC, America’s foremost genocide lobby. Who would have guessed?

There’s no paywall in this edition of ‘First Draft,’ thanks to our sponsor Ground News.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we dive into the Michigan primary, a shocking fifth accuser precedes Eric Swalwell’s resignation from Congress, and Donald Trump’s war in Iran bites deep on the home front.

Michigan’s Democratic primary for US Senate is a three-way dead heat, according to a new poll conducted for Zeteo and Drop Site News.

The survey additionally found that likely Democratic primary voters are far more concerned about the pro-Israel lobby AIPAC’s influence on candidates than the influence of Hasan Piker, the leftist Twitch streamer who campaigned with candidate Abdul El-Sayed at two Michigan universities last week.

The poll from Data for Progress found that 22% of likely Democratic primary voters support El-Sayed, endorsed by Bernie Sanders and a vocal critic of Israel; 23% support Haley Stevens, a conservative, pro-Israel Democrat backed by party leaders in Washington, DC; and 22% support Mallory McMorrow, the J Street-endorsed candidate positioning herself in between Stevens and El-Sayed.

Notably, a third of voters were undecided.

The poll also tested what would happen in a two-way race, and found that McMorrow would benefit more from El-Sayed dropping out than vice versa: His voters are more likely to consider McMorrow their second choice, whereas McMorrow’s are more likely to call Stevens their second choice, suggesting that McMorrow is not simply splitting the progressive vote.

However, in all of the scenarios that were polled, a plurality of likely Democratic primary voters remained unsure as to whom they would support – suggesting, again, that the race remains fully up for grabs with three-and-a-half months left before the Aug. 4 primary election.

Meanwhile, voters are not overly concerned about El-Sayed’s decision to hold rallies with Piker – despite the aggressive anti-Piker cancellation campaign led by the centrist think tank Third Way, egged on by both McMorrow and Stevens.

Per the poll, 13% of likely Democratic primary voters in Michigan view Piker favorably, while 7% view him negatively. The vast majority of them say they don’t know enough about Piker to have an opinion about him.

The poll gave voters more context about the controversy to assess whether it would influence their vote. They were told that El-Sayed’s opponents had criticized him for planning rallies with Piker, accusing Piker of antisemitism and highlighting past controversial remarks, and that El-Sayed has argued that criticism of Israel should not be confused with antisemitism.

Asked then whether they approved or disapproved of El-Sayed inviting Piker to join his rallies on college campuses, 40% said they approved, 30% said they disapproved, and 30% said they weren’t sure.

Michigan primary voters appear significantly more concerned about the influence of AIPAC, America’s top pro-Israel lobby, with far less prodding: 64% said they are less likely to support a Senate candidate who receives donations from AIPAC and other pro-Israel groups, while 10% said they are more likely.

The poll found 62% of Michigan Democratic primary voters agreed that they are less likely to trust that a candidate would stand up for Michiganders in general if they won’t stand up to AIPAC; 13% disagreed with this sentiment.

Data for Progress polled 515 likely Democratic primary voters in Michigan, from April 2-8.

Stevens has long been an AIPAC favorite. AIPAC’s political action committee raised over $340,000 worth of direct campaign donations for Stevens in early 2025, just before she announced her bid for Senate, though it has not raised money directly for her Senate campaign. Last month, Stevens taped a promotional video for AIPAC, which in 2022 helped her oust former Michigan Democratic Rep. Andy Levin, a progressive Zionist who argued for Palestinian rights.

El-Sayed and McMorrow have both been critical of Israel’s genocide in Gaza, though McMorrow has been reluctant to describe it this way – and has instead complained that the word “genocide” has become some kind of “political purity test.”

As Drop Site News previously reported, McMorrow privately drafted a position paper for AIPAC, according to one of her supporters, before publicly demanding a permanent ceasefire in Gaza and saying that “this violence needs to stop.”

McMorrow has been endorsed by the liberal pro-Israel lobby J Street.

When it comes to stories as complex as the one you just saw about the Michigan Democratic primary, sometimes it helps to get an additional perspective.

That’s where Ground News comes in. Ground News is a news app and website that scans 50,000+ articles while offering built-in bias comparisons, fact checks, and funding transparency for every source.

Look up any news story, and Ground News will break down its current coverage. For each source, you can also see key details such as political leaning, the outlet’s track record of factuality, and who owns it.

With Ground News’ Vantage Plan, information becomes your superpower. Learn more at ground.news/zeteox and get 40% off the Vantage Plan for just $5 a month – cheaper than a cup of morning coffee.

(This portion of the post was sponsored content)

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s Human Rights Activists News Agency said last week the death toll from US-Israeli strikes stands at more than 3,000, including 1,700 civilians.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 2,124 people, including at least 168 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 34 people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Talks hint: Donald Trump hinted that new talks with Iran may happen “over the next two days” in Pakistan. He previously said the US would not return for more talks in the country, after the first failed round.

Strait open: The first day of Trump’s blockade of Iranian ports made little difference to traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, Reuters reported, saying shipping data showed at least three Iran-linked tankers crossed the waterway.

Over/under: Emerging from the White House, Fox host Maria Bartiromo said Trump told her the war with Iran was “over.” The full interview will run on Wednesday.

Home front: Republican Senator Roger Marshall had an odd message for Americans opposed to Trump’s war: “I’m sorry that gas prices are going up, but… your national security is even more important than your pocketbook.”

Farm crisis: According to the American Farm Bureau Federation, 70% of US farmers cannot afford enough fertilizer to get them through the year. Fertilizer is just one commodity hit by conditions in the Strait of Hormuz.

Lebanon latest: Israeli and Lebanese officials held their first direct talks in more than 30 years in Washington, despite Benjamin Netanyahu initially opposing the move. Both countries said talks were productive and were expected to continue. Meanwhile, Israel issued new forced evacuation orders to residents in the southern part of the country.

After Lonna Drewes became the fifth woman to accuse Eric Swalwell of sexual misconduct, the California Democrat formally resigned from the US House. Appearing before reporters in Beverly Hills, Drewes, a model who works in fashion technology, said the former gubernatorial candidate drugged, choked, and raped her in 2018.

“My delay in taking action against Eric was driven by fear, not doubt,” Drewes said. “Fear of his political power, his background as an attorney, and his family law enforcement ties.”

A lawyer for Swalwell said he “categorically and unequivocally denies each and every allegation,” but he quit Congress all the same. So did Tony Gonzales, a Texas Republican who admitted to an affair with an aide who died by suicide.

Pro-sedition president: Trump’s DOJ wants to vacate the seditious conspiracy convictions of Proud Boys and Oath Keepers Jan. 6 leaders, whose sentences were commuted.

Speak now: House Speaker Mike Johnson, a devout Christian, said he asked Trump to take down an AI image depicting himself as Jesus, which Trump claimed to think showed him as a doctor.

Court move: Trump refused to concede defeat in his suit against the Wall Street Journal, seeking to find if the paper acted with actual malice in reporting on his lewd birthday letter to Jeffrey Epstein.

Amend to that: Rep. Jamie Raskin introduced a bill to form a commission to study whether Trump should be removed under the 25th Amendment.

Repay day: A refund process for payments made before Trump’s tariffs were made illegal begins April 20, officials said. Actual repayments may take longer.

Sparse crowd: JD Vance spoke at a Turning Point event in Athens, Georgia. Not many people turned up to listen.

Spy play: Republicans continued to tie themselves in knots over whether to reauthorize surveillance powers that allow warrantless wiretaps of US citizens.

Trump turns again: Trump lashed out at Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni, one of his closest European allies, after she called his attacks on the pope “unacceptable,” and after her government suspended a defense agreement with Israel. “Giorgia Meloni doesn’t want to help us in the war,” Trump told an Italian newspaper. “I’m shocked at her. I thought she had courage, but I was wrong.”

Presidents have used the 25th Amendment to temporarily transfer power four times. In 1985, Ronald Reagan empowered George H.W. Bush for eight hours while he had cancer surgery. What procedure necessitated two transfers under George W Bush and one under Joe Biden?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Hamas reiterated its opposition to the disarmament plan put forth by the so-called “Board of Peace,” citing Israeli ceasefire violations. Israel conducted targeted airstrikes throughout Gaza, killing at least 11 Palestinians, including two children.

🇰🇼 Unjust detainment: Human rights advocates called for the release of journalist Ahmed Shihab-Eldin, held in Kuwait for six weeks after publishing photos and videos of a fighter jet crash, as Gulf countries crack down on coverage of the war with Iran.

🇸🇴 Deadly tensions: Somalia’s army carried out a military operation, with assistance from international partners, in the autonomous state of Jubbaland, killing 27 al-Shabab fighters and seizing weapons and equipment.

🇮🇪 Price outrage: After Irish haulers, farmers, and agricultural workers blocked oil infrastructure in response to rising fuel prices, two lawmakers withdrew from the parliamentary coalition in power.

Tim: For the record, gas prices are higher now than they were when Joe Biden left office. But of course, you and the rest of the GOP will recycle that line all the way to November, cause you have no excuse for Donald Trump hurting folks at the pump.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

Thank you for being a paid subscriber to Zeteo, your support helps keep this media movement alive. If you’d like to wear your support, don’t forget to check out

For any issues with your subscription, please email us at info@zeteonews.com.

items.title:
'Do You Accept Israel Has Nuclear Weapons?' Mehdi DEBATES Israeli Military
 Spokesman
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
Do you get annoyed when Israeli officials go on television and obsess over Iran’s (non-existent) nukes while getting a pass on their own actual nuclear weapons stockpile? Do you wonder why no one challenges them on their hypocrisy and lawlessness?

Well, Mehdi has you covered in the latest episode of ‘Piers Uncensored.’

Zeteo’s editor-in-chief debated Israeli military spokesman Doron Spielman on everything from uranium enrichment in Iran under the JCPOA to last week’s 10-minute massacre of Lebanese civilians to, yes, Israel’s secret stash of nuclear warheads in defiance of international law.

You’ll be shocked to hear it got heated. And you’ll also be shocked to hear that Mehdi brought receipts.

On Israel’s nukes:

Spielman: “You and I both know there are no binding UN Security Council resolutions against Israel.”

Mehdi: “Yes there is. UN Security Council Resolution 487. You’re wrong, Doron…. you’re lying… you can Google it right now”

On Israel’s attack on Lebanon:

Mehdi: “UN peacekeepers in southern Lebanon say Israel violated the ceasefire, not a thousand times, not 5,000 times, they say you violated the ceasefire 15,000 times over the last 18 months.”

Spielman: “I don’t know about your numbers…”

Mehdi: “Google is your friend, Doron.”

Watch the full episode above and do please consider becoming a paid subscriber to Zeteo and/or a donor to Zeteo.

Catch up on Zeteo’s latest stories:

Do you wonder why no one challenges them on their hypocrisy and lawlessness? Well, Mehdi has you covered in the latest episode…

items.title:
Prominent Journalist Ahmed Shihab-Eldin Detained in Kuwait as Gulf Cracks Down
 on Free Speech During Iran War
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
Human rights advocates are calling for Kuwait to immediately release prominent Kuwaiti-American journalist Ahmed Shihab-Eldin after he was arrested six weeks ago amid a wartime crackdown on press and speech.

Shihab-Eldin, a US citizen of Palestinian descent known for his excoriating commentary on Israel, was arrested in early March and has been in Kuwaiti detention since then without public justification. The Emmy-nominated journalist has previously worked with PBS, the New York Times, and Al Jazeera English.

“We call on Kuwait to release Ahmed Shihab-Eldin and drop all charges against him. Journalism is not a crime, and Shihab-Eldin’s case reflects a broader pattern of using national security laws to stifle scrutiny and control the narrative,” Sara Qudah, the Committee to Protect Journalists MENA regional director, said in an emailed statement.

Qudah told Zeteo over the phone that Shihab-Eldin was on a personal trip visiting family when he was arrested. On March 2, Shihab-Eldin shared photos and videos that had been published by CNN or were circulating online that showed a fighter jet crash over Kuwait near a US air base. The posts appeared to show a friendly fire incident where Kuwaiti air defenses shot down three US planes. No pilots were killed. Shihab-Eldin has not posted on social media since March 2.

Six weeks into his detention, Qudah said Shihab-Eldin’s charges have still not been made public, and Kuwaiti authorities have not responded to CPJ’s questions about Shihab-Eldin’s condition in prison and the grounds for his arrest. But she said it appears authorities have charged him with spreading false information, harming national security, and misusing his phone.

Shortly after the war in Iran began, Kuwait’s Information Ministry published media “guidelines,” which include a ban on publishing or circulating “rumors, unverified news, or misleading information” or content that could offend “brotherly or friendly countries or that could harm Kuwait’s foreign relations.”

CPJ called the unconfirmed charges “overly broad accusations that are routinely used to silence independent journalists.” The charges, if confirmed, carry a penalty of up to 10 years in prison, Qudah said.

UN special rapporteur Francesca Albanese condemned the arrest on Tuesday, writing on social media that “journalism is not a crime.”

“One of the most gentle souls this genocide has brought into my life is Ahmed Shihab-Eldin, a journalist of rare strength, unwavering integrity, and deep compassion,” Albanese said.

Gulf-Wide Crackdown

Shihab-Eldin’s imprisonment comes as Gulf countries crack down on press freedoms. Across the Gulf, Qudah said hundreds of citizens have been arrested in recent weeks, simply for posting or reposting information on the war.

“Journalists are not being able to freely and safely report on what is happening inside their countries and in where they are working, which undermines their jobs and … what the public needs in terms of receiving credible and factual information,” Qudah said.

In Kuwait specifically, authorities have already made dozens of arbitrary arrests over social media posts, according to the Gulf Centre for Human Rights. Sources reportedly confirmed to the organization that most of the people arrested were held in secret prisons for several days and were denied contact with their families or lawyers – a common tool of intimidation.

Recent arrests in Kuwait include Fareah Alsaqqaf and Suad Al-Munayes, two activists currently in custody without access to attorneys or clear information on the basis for their arrest, according to HuMENA. Yitzchak Horowitz, a journalist with the ultra-Orthodox Israeli magazine, BeKehila, reported late last month that he had been arrested in Kuwait on suspicion of espionage after taking photos of US warships off the Gulf Coast. Horowitz said he was questioned for several hours before being released.

Former adviser to the UK’s Middle East peace envoy, Ashish Prashar, told Zeteo that arbitrarily imprisoning journalists under the guise of national security is a “grave misstep.”

“Journalism is fundamentally about telling the truth; it is essential work, an act of service, and one the public needs in order to make informed decisions,” Prashar said. “The State Department and Foreign Office need to demand the release of all media workers from their allies in the Gulf. Anything less would be an abandonment of their ethical obligations and an endorsement of authoritarian practices.”

The State Department and the Kuwaiti Embassy in Washington, DC, did not immediately return a request for comment.

Have a tip for Minnah? Reach out on Signal at minnahfarshad.94.

Check out more from Zeteo:
