At least 738 Palestinians have been slaughtered, children continue to starve, and the already dire humanitarian…

items.title: 🙋‍♀️ Melania, Out of the Blue, Denies Ties to Epstiein
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 1919, Mexican revolutionary Emiliano Zapata was shot dead by government forces. Decades later, Zapata’s image was used by the United Farm Workers, a US group jointly led by Dolores Huerta, the great campaigner who turns 96 today.

Good morning! Andrew and Swin here. Our president continues to be racist, ICE keeps shooting people, and the war in Iran could restart at any moment. But this morning, our lede is about Melania Trump and Jeffrey Epstein. Don’t blame us. It was her idea.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ the first lady volunteers that she “was never involved” in Epstein’s crimes, Democrats can’t pass a symbolic resolution calling out AIPAC, and Donald Trump doesn’t seem happy with his ceasefire deal, even after he decided Israel could keep massacring people in Lebanon.

‘What the Fuck Is She Doing?’

Polls show a majority of Americans believe that Donald Trump launched his illegal and reckless war in Iran to distract from his Jeffrey Epstein scandal.

But on Thursday, first lady Melania Trump made the bizarre decision to hold a surprise press event, so she could draw attention away from the worst-run war in US history and bring the president’s ties to the world’s most famous pedophile right back into the national news cycle.

“I’m not a witness or a named witness in connection with any of Epstein’s crimes,” Melania Trump declared. “My name has never appeared in court documents, depositions, victim statements, or FBI interviews surrounding the Epstein matter. I have never had any knowledge of Epstein’s abuse of his victims. I was never involved in any capacity.”

She continued: “I was not a participant, was never on Epstein’s plane, and never visited his private island. I have never been legally accused or convinced [sic] of a crime in connection with Epstein’s sex trafficking, abuse of minors, and other repulsive behavior.”

Melania Trump’s comments reminded Swin of this clip of Frank from ‘It’s Always Sunny in Philadelphia.’ It reminded Andrew just slightly more of this classic meme.

“What the fuck is she doing right now?” one close Trump adviser asked us shortly after Melania Trump stopped speaking.

“I’ve never been friends with Epstein,” Melania Trump said. “Donald and I were invited to the same parties as Epstein from time to time, since overlapping in social circles is common in New York City and Palm Beach. To be clear, I never had a relationship with Epstein or his accomplice, [Ghislaine] Maxwell.”

This was a televised performance that no one was asking for, and which few could immediately explain.

Was she just mad about random commenters online? Was she trying to front-run some major piece of investigative journalism or tabloid fodder for which her office or lawyer had received a comment request? These weren’t just questions that we here at Zeteo had; similar questions abounded in Trumpland, almost as soon as the event began.

Donald Trump, for his part, told MS Now that he didn’t “know anything about” his wife’s statement before she started speaking. Speaking about Epstein, he added: “She didn’t know him.”

Two senior administration officials said they had no idea that the first lady was going to do this press event (though it’s unclear if they would have gotten a heads-up for that sort of thing to begin with), and they were similarly baffled as to why she did it.

One Republican who’s been close to Donald and Melania Trump for years simply said that they found her comments to be a “poorly worded denial.” Speaking about the first lady’s insistence that she never had a relationship with Epstein, this source stated bluntly: “I would have gone a different route.”

In another interesting decision, Melania appeared to either suggest or confirm that she’s in the Epstein files.

“My email reply to Maxwell cannot be categorized as anything more than casual correspondence,” she said. “My polite reply to her email doesn’t amount to anything more than a trivial note.“

As part of its Epstein files dump, the Justice Department released copies of an email exchange in which someone named “Melania” (the sender is redacted) writes to a “G. Max” in 2002: “Dear G! How are you? Nice story about JE in NY mag. You look great on the picture. I know you are very busy flying all over the world. How was Palm Beach? I cannot wait to go down. Give me a call when you are back in NY. Have a great time! Love, Melania.”

The New York magazine story about Epstein released around that time is… yes, the one where Donald Trump famously said, “I’ve known Jeff for 15 years. Terrific guy. He’s a lot of fun to be with. It is even said that he likes beautiful women as much as I do, and many of them are on the younger side. No doubt about it – Jeffrey enjoys his social life.” (The story also includes a picture of Epstein and Maxwell.)

The DOJ documents show “G. Max” replied to “Melania” some time later: “Sweet pea – thanks for your message. Actually plans changed again and I am now on my way back to NY. I leave again on Fri so I still do not think I have time to see you sadly. I will try and call though.”

The White House and Justice Department did not immediately respond to Zeteo’s email seeking clarification as to whether this was the “casual correspondence” with Maxwell that the first lady referred to in her surprise press conference.

Melania Trump ended her speech with a call for Congress “to provide the women who have been victimized by Epstein with a public hearing.”

“Give these victims their opportunity to testify under oath in front of Congress,” she said adding: “Each and every woman should have her day to tell her story in public, if she wishes, and then her testimony should be permanently entered into the Congressional Record.”

What does Donald Trump think about that? Is his war going that badly?

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s deputy health minister told Al Jazeera that at least 1,937 people had been killed in US-Israeli attacks. Iranian officials said last month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 1,530 people, including at least 130 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 33 people, including 10 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Dire Strait: Trump ranted repeatedly on Truth Social about Iran’s control of the Strait of Hormuz, casting further doubt on his own faltering ceasefire.

Fateful call: Trump and other stakeholders believed the ceasefire included Lebanon, CBS News reported, only for “the US position [to] shift … following a phone call” between Benjamin Netanyahu and the US president. Israel continued attacking, including striking Lebanon more than 100 times in 10 minutes. After killing more than 300 in the deadliest day of its attacks so far, Netanyahu claimed Israel wanted talks with Beirut.

Try, try again: Ex-Secretary of State John Kerry said Netanyahu asked Presidents Bush, Obama, and Biden to strike Iran and was rebuffed each time.

Cadets marooned: Cadets from the US Merchant Marine Academy got stuck in the Persian Gulf at the start of the war because the Pentagon did not warn their school, their vessels, or the Department of Transportation, NOTUS said.

Dems denied: House Republicans blocked an attempt to use a pro forma session to pass a war powers resolution and rein in Trump on Iran.

Post haste: In a typically insane, dishonest, near-500-word screed, Trump ranted about Tucker Carlson, Alex Jones, Megyn Kelly, and Candace Owens, far-right independent media personalities now against him on Iran. He called them “low-IQ” and pretended never to have been big fans of theirs!

Starmer is ‘fed up’: UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer said today he is “fed up” with rising energy prices “because of the actions of Putin or Trump across the world.” Is he also “fed up” because the US-Israeli war on Iran and Israeli strikes on Lebanon have killed thousands??

Dems Can’t Quit AIPAC

A Democratic National Committee panel rejected a resolution to criticize AIPAC’s primary spending – the latest sign of the party’s refusal to walk away from the pro-Israel lobby.

At a DNC meeting in New Orleans, a committee voted on nearly three dozen resolutions, on subjects including opposing ICE brutality and condemning redistricting and dark money.

One resolution the committee voted down called for the DNC to affirm that elections should “reflect grassroots participation and the will of voters, rather than the disproportionate influence of wealthy donors of special interests.” It called out AIPAC, which spent $126 million in the 2024 cycle and more than $20 million in last month’s Illinois primaries, aiming to install allies of Israel in the US government.

DNC chair Ken Martin defended the no vote by saying the panel approved a resolution broadly condemning dark money in politics.

Another resolution called for the DNC to recognize Palestinian statehood and demand an immediate ceasefire in Gaza. The panel opted not to put it to a vote, saying a similar motion had been introduced at an earlier meeting and that a “task force” was working on it.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

Obscene post: Trump posted what he said was a video of a Florida murder by a Haitian immigrant, accompanied by racist and inflammatory invective. He claimed he had “an obligation” to show the footage.

ICE shooting: A man in California was wounded at least six times when agents opened fire, his lawyer said, adding that Carlos Ivan Mendoza Hernandez, from El Salvador, had at least one gunshot wound to his jaw and another on his arm.

Good news: Federal prosecutors must give a judge records concerning Jonathan Ross, the ICE agent who killed Renee Nicole Good in Minneapolis in January. The order is in a separate case but covers records relating to Good’s death.

Court blows: A federal judge slammed Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth for violating an order to restore full Pentagon press access. Another judge denied an administration attempt to search devices owned by a Washington Post reporter.

Sanders moves: Bernie Sanders and three allies filed Senate resolutions to stop arms sales to Israel worth nearly $650 million. Sanders also announced a New York event on Sunday, with Mayor Zohran Mamdani, to launch a new group, Union Now.

Tough ask: Olivia Reingold of the Free Press said Michigan Senate hopeful Abdul El-Sayed “rebuffed” her question about “Israel’s right to exist as a Jewish state” and “narrow[ed] his eyes” as he did so. Video showed Reingold failed to explain what she meant before misquoting El-Sayed, who did not appear to “narrow his eyes.” The Free Press corrected the misquote but left the eyes line in.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

Which actor, an Oscar winner for portraying a notorious serial killer, played Megyn Kelly in ‘Bombshell,’ a 2019 thriller about sexual harassment at Fox?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israel carried out airstrikes in Gaza City and Khan Younis, killing three Palestinians and injuring several, according to the Wafa agency.

🇷🇺 Flood response: Russian ministers surveyed flooding damage in Dagestan after days-long torrential rain left six people dead and thousands displaced.

🇳🇬 Base attack: A general and several soldiers were killed in Nigeria’s Borno state, in a coordinated attack by Boko Haram and Islamic State West Africa Province.

🇫🇷 Boat rescue: Four migrants died while attempting to board an inflatable boat traveling from France to the UK. Thirty-eight were rescued by French authorities.

😳 WTF?!

Ahead of negotiations, Ted Cruz is doubling down on Trump’s threats of genocide against Iran. Let’s make sure he and Lindsey Graham are kept as far away from the Situation Room as possible.

🧠 Trivia answer: Charlize Theron (who played Eileen Wurnos in ‘Monster,’ from 2003)

📖 Weekend Reads

How Trump Took the US to War With Iran

From mid-to-late February, the Situation Room became the hub for intense conversations between Trump and his inner circle, as the president contemplated whether he should conduct a military attack on Iran – an idea pitched by Benjamin Netanyahu. Jonathan Swan and Maggie Haberman of the New York Times provide the inside scoop on what went down, ultimately leading to Trump waging war.

I Watched Right-Wing Journalists Try to Shame Abdul El-Sayed and Hasan Piker

Mainstream media spent the week trying to take down streamer Hasan Piker. But as Piker campaigned with Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed at Michigan colleges, it was clear the cancellation campaign had little effect on voters, with the two turning out hundreds. Zeteo’s Minnah Arshad attended the rallies and reports on what many journalists are failing to cover: that Piker has been an asset – not a liability – for El-Sayed’s campaign.

What if Democrats Didn’t Suck? With Morris Katz

The Democratic Party is experiencing a major identity crisis as it attempts to strategize a response to Trump’s attacks on our democracy. In this episode of The Nation’s ‘Fighting Facism’ podcast, political strategist Morris Katz, who’s advised Zohran Mamdani and Graham Platner, dives into how Democrats can embrace economic populism in order to earn back the trust of voters ahead of the midterms and 2028.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly, Akshay Gokul, and Minnah Arshad contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

Our president…

items.title: 'I Can’t Forget the Smell': Lebanese Reel After Israel Kills Over 300 in Single Day
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
This article was published in collaboration with Egab.

BEIRUT, Lebanon – The smell was the first thing Louna couldn’t explain. Charred flesh, gunpowder, burnt fabric, and the rubber of ambulance tires screaming down the steep entrance ramp to the hospital. She had worked through the Beirut port explosion. She had worked through the pager attacks. Nothing had smelled like this.

“I don’t know what kind of rockets they used,” said Louna, 30, an emergency room nurse at a hospital in Beirut’s suburbs. “I can’t forget the smell, it was so strong.”

At around 2:20 in the afternoon, the hospital heard the strikes and rang the Code Orange alert. Louna called her father first – she knew he was out on the streets – then her mother, her sister, the rest of her family. They were all crying. She prayed, walking toward the ER, that it wasn’t as bad as it sounded.

The first patients arrived in shock, eyes wide, unable to speak. Most were from Hay Al-Sillom in Beirut’s southern suburbs. They had glass embedded in their legs, their backs, and their heads. They didn’t understand what had happened to them. Then five bodies came in, charred beyond recognition. The only way to identify them was by a ring, a watch, or eventually through DNA and dental records.

An elderly man arrived looking for his son, tall, in his thirties, wearing olive-colored trousers. Staff directed him to the morgue. He left without going, still hoping to find his son alive somewhere else.

When the shift was over, the team went to the break room and wept.

“None of us could sleep last night,” Louna said. “The image of those bodies, and that smell, it wouldn’t leave us.”

More than 100 Israeli strikes hit Lebanon in 10 minutes on Wednesday in an operation dubbed by the Israeli military as “Eternal Darkness.” It was the heaviest bombardment Beirut had absorbed since the 1980s, according to the Lebanese Civil Defense officials on the ground. And it came without warning.

The Health Ministry said on Thursday that the preliminary death toll from Wednesday alone is at least 303 people, including 110 women, children, and elderly people. More than 1,100 others were injured. The death toll since Israel renewed its escalation in Lebanon on March 2 is now at least 1,888, while 6,092 have been injured, according to the country’s crises management unit WhatsApp channel.

The timing of Wednesday’s bombardment was deliberate in its cruelty: midday, with schools reopening after Easter break, offices emptying for lunch, streets at their most crowded.

Many people in the city had woken up that morning to news of ceasefire talks and decided it was safe to go outside. When the first strikes sounded, most in Beirut assumed they were hitting the southern suburbs. The Israeli army had been issuing warnings for that area throughout the war, and the city had learned to calibrate its fear accordingly.

Then the strikes started landing in the city itself. Corniche Al-Mazraa. Basta, Mouseitbeh, Bashoura, Tallet Al-Khayat. Shweifat. Al-Rihab. Jnah and Bir Hasan. Most of these neighborhoods had not been hit with such ferocity in this war or the one before it. Because of that, hundreds of thousands of forcibly displaced people had taken shelter in these areas on the unspoken assumption that they were beyond reach. They were not.

“We will continue striking the Hezbollah terror organization and will utilize every operational opportunity. We will not compromise the security of the residents of northern Israel. We will continue to strike with determination,” the Israeli military said in a tweet.

But the people who died and were terrorized weren’t combatants. They were mothers, fathers, sons, daughters, colleagues, and friends. They were attending a funeral, or at work, or simply sitting at home.

‘She Just Wanted to Raise Her Children’

Jamila Hashim could not read or write. She knew other things instead, how to clean a stranger’s house so thoroughly they asked her back, how to cook food people remembered, how to keep three children fed on the wages of her own two hands after the company that employed her husband refused to pay out his death benefits following a workplace accident three years ago.

Her youngest daughter, who is 6 years old, has a developmental disability and is frightened by loud sounds. It was for her, more than anyone, that Jamila left her southern village of Ansar when the shelling began.

She tried the city of Jbeil. She tried Zgharta. She eventually settled in Beirut’s Bechara El-Khoury neighborhood. She cleaned houses in the mornings and cooked for families in the evenings, and she did not ask for help.

Her 10-year-old son had started taking on small errands to contribute. Her 9-year-old daughter had learned to manage the household on the days her mother worked double shifts. The youngest stayed close, startled at every sound.

On Wednesday afternoon, a strike hit Corniche Al-Mazraa, a few streets away. Jamila was killed. Her three children are alone now.

“She never asked anyone for anything,” said Maha, a colleague who had worked alongside her husband before his death. “She just worked and wanted to raise her children with dignity.”

A Funeral that Led to More Funerals

In the Northern Beqaa town of Shmistar, what is left of the villagers gathered to bury a man in his sixties who died a day earlier.

After noon prayers, the men were heading to bury the deceased when the rockets showered them. Ten men were killed instantly, and around 35 people were injured. The cemetery was hit hard. Graves were open, and bodies were scattered all over beyond recognition, according to a resident.

“I can’t even describe what happened. Only God protected my brother, who was at the funeral. He returned home traumatized, covered with blood and soot and injured by fragments,” Lama Tlais, 29, told me. They rushed her brother Khalil, 20, to the hospital to get treatment for his injuries, and he was discharged on Thursday, but he is still in shock.

On Thursday, the village gathered again to bury its dead.

‘I Don’t Know How We Survived’

After the strikes, around 35 people went missing, several of them children, according to the Information Minister Paul Morkos.

Sharif Mubarak, 34, doesn’t remember leaving the building. One moment, he was inside his flat on the third floor of the building in the Bir Hasan neighborhood in Beirut with his wife, his two daughters, Mariam, 6, and Malika, 4, and his 10-month-old son. Then the strike hit, and whatever came next, how they moved, which direction, how they got out, is gone from his memory.

“The blast took our hearing and their ability to think at the same time, he said. Everything went dark at once,” he told me.

When awareness came back, there was blood everywhere, and smoke, and screaming. The building was partially destroyed. He was taken to the Rafik Hariri Public Hospital. His wife was taken to another hospital with minor injuries. His daughters were not with him, and he didn’t know where they were. For hours, they were missing. His own injuries were relatively minor, but the not-knowing was its own kind of wound.

Hours later, after photos of the girls circulated, a relative found him and told him he had taken them to his nearby home. They were safe.

Sharif is still at the hospital. His 10-month-old son swallowed a large amount of dust and was found under the rubble. The baby may be discharged on Friday or the day after. He doesn’t know where they will go when that happens.

“I don’t know how my entire family survived,” he said. “It feels like a miracle inside all this destruction.”

It will be his second displacement. The first time, he left his home in the southern village of Dweir. He doesn’t yet know where the second one ends.

Ali Awada is a Lebanese journalist who has covered Lebanese affairs for more than a decade for regional and international outlets.

items.description: This article was published in collaboration with Egab. BEIRUT, Lebanon – The smell was the first thing Louna couldn’t explain. Charred flesh, gunpowder, burnt fabric, and the rubber of ambulance tires screaming down the steep entrance ramp to the hospital. She had worked through the Beirut port…

items.title: This Is a Republican-Made Disaster
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
Tune in

Zeteo contributor John Harwood is hosting a Substack live at 1:50pm ET Friday (10:50am PT, 6:50pm BST) with economist Justin Wolfers on how Trump is making the US poorer. They’ll take your questions.

Americans have begun to sense the scale of the catastrophe that Donald Trump’s second presidency has inflicted on all of us.

The year-one decimation of government functions, self-harm of high tariffs, brutality on city streets, and broad assault on the rule of law were bad enough. Now the mentally unbalanced commander in chief publicly threatens monstrous crimes in an ineptly-conceived war, trashing America’s global leadership while constituents suffer the economic consequences – among which spiking gas prices represent just the opening blow.

Trump seeks to corrupt this fall’s midterm elections that might constrain him. So far, he has failed.

That leaves a simple political question for 2026: whether American voters hold accountable the parties who caused this nightmare. Or, more precisely, the party – the Republican Party.

Yes, Trump is the madman abusing power. But the GOP has empowered him, long after the dangers became apparent.

In the history of America’s two-party system, rarely has one borne singular responsibility for so gravely damaging our country and the world. Ideological heterogeneity, geographic variation, and divided government usually disperse accountability and blame.

Not now. Republicans own this mess as unmistakably as the 19th-century Democrats who nearly shattered the United States to preserve slavery.

“The Civil War would be the only one that would even come close,” says Princeton University historian Kevin Kruse. The new Republican Party led by Abraham Lincoln saved the union then. Those parties bear no resemblance to Republicans and Democrats today.

Here, in an account of modern-day Republican culpability, the both-sides conventions of journalism might label this the mirror-image of a comparable modern Democratic failure. But it is not.

Have Democrats erased the economic pain accompanying globalization? Have they figured out how to protect the rights and dignity of transgender Americans without offending the prerogatives and sensibilities of others? Have they found a politically sustainable balance for orderly immigration under law?

No.

Have Democrats nominated the most compelling candidates lately?

Also no.

Hillary Clinton evoked viscerally negative reactions from many voters. Kamala Harris did not display firm convictions. Joe Biden was too old for an effective second term.

But their shortcomings were trivial alongside the Trump menace. And all three accurately warned the American people about him..

Biden sometimes struggled for words and walked unsteadily. Trump cannot think clearly and rationally, absorb information, or control his destructive impulses.

In 2015, President Barack Obama joined allies to forge a diplomatic agreement with Iran restraining its nuclear program. Over objections from his first-term Defense Secretary Jim Mattis, Trump junked it and ultimately set the stage for this war.

In acquiescing across the board, the GOP’s failure of judgment, courage, and morality has no modern precedent. Nor did it require seeing Trump in office to understand the danger ahead.

After his own 2016 presidential bid faltered, Senator Ted Cruz called Trump a “pathological liar” unable to discern reality. Then-Senator Marco Rubio called him “an erratic individual” who could not be trusted with nuclear weapons.

“He’s a con man,” Rubio said. “I will never stop until we keep a con man from taking over the party of Reagan and the conservative movement.”

Rubio stopped, in exchange for power. The entire Republican Party stopped. Scattered resisters typically wait until they walk out the door.

Retiring Senator Mitch McConnell now opposes Trump’s most egregiously unfit nominees and publicly rejects the White House threat to abandon NATO. Following Jan. 6, as Senate Republican Leader, he might have foreclosed all of this by rallying votes to convict Trump on impeachment charges. He shrank from the risk.

Senator Bill Cassidy now says Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s anti-vaccine crusade will “make America sicker.” Last year, Cassidy could have blocked Kennedy’s confirmation as Health and Human Services Secretary. He also shrank under pressure.

So have scores of other Republican senators who agreed to install Trump’s fanatical wrecking crew and now scramble to shield constituents from harm. They stay silent as Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth derides democratic allies, fires competent military officers, and invokes religion to glorify killing Iranians as ardently as an Islamic jihadist.

Timorous GOP lawmakers have refused to assert their constitutional power to authorize the war. They have also blocked Democratic efforts to stop it.

Trump’s deranged howling on social media – demanding that “crazy bastards” in Iran “Open the Fuckin’ Strait (of Hormuz)” or “a whole civilization will die” – left GOP officeholders ducking for cover before the president backed down to accept a two-week ceasefire. A few “formers” spoke out.

“He has gone insane, and all of you are complicit,” declared Trump’s one-time House ally Marjorie Taylor Greene, calling on administration officials to “intervene in Trump’s madness.”

“More people now should be calling for this man’s removal” under the Constitution’s 25th Amendment, added ex-Trump communications director Anthony Scaramucci.

That will not happen. But voters will soon choose a new House and Senate.

Trump’s sub-40% public approval almost guarantees a Democratic takeover of the House. Their Senate prospects remain dicier, but Republicans must defend 22 seats they now hold.

No one knows how many Democrats can flip. According to independent pollster G. Elliott Morris, one-fourth of American adults can’t identify which party controls Congress; predicting their votes isn’t easy.

We can, however, easily identify who has imperiled our country so severely. With both arms and all 10 fingers, the Republican Party is holding the bag.

John Harwood is the former chief Washington correspondent for CNBC and White House correspondent for CNN. He has interviewed every president from George H.W. Bush to Joe Biden. Sign up for ‘The Stakes with John Harwood’ to get all of his columns in your inbox.

The views expressed in this article are the author’s own and do not necessarily reflect those of Zeteo.

items.title: 👀 Furious Trump Launches Huge Hunt Over Iran Leaks
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 1866, Gen. Ulysses S Grant was stopped for speeding in a horse-drawn buggy on 14th Street in Washington, DC. In 2012, the then-chief of DC police said Grant was arrested in such fashion three times, twice while president – shameful, but one fewer arrest than endured by his current successor in office, Donald J Trump.

Hi! It’s Swin again.

The Donald Trump-shaped apocalypse has been postponed, even if his war goes on. With it comes new horrors each day, at home and abroad. Amid it all, the president of the United States is falling back on a familiar habit: huffily ordering aides to bring him the heads (figuratively speaking… we hope) of the people who’ve been snitching on him. So let’s get into that.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Trump demands action as a new book looms, JD Vance tries to explain away the ceasefire that wasn’t, amid Israeli savagery in Lebanon, and Pam Bondi tries to bail on a date to discuss Jeffrey Epstein before the House Oversight Committee.

The President Smells a Rat

Donald Trump is a Mad King president, perpetually pissed off about seemingly everything. That is always what you like to hear about someone running a major new war that’s killing thousands and affecting millions across multiple continents. But there’s something new that is uniquely enraging him, those who’ve spoken to him recently tell me. In conversations with close aides and advisers, President Trump has loudly demanded to know who in his Cabinet or his team blabbed to New York Times reporters Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan for their forthcoming book, Regime Change – including over his reckless decision to launch an illegal war against Iran.

In response to the president’s demands, Trump officials have launched a massive leak hunt within Trumpland and the federal government, multiple Trump advisers say.

Some of the presidential allies and government officials I spoke to are genuinely worried about what could happen to some of the book’s sources – in or out of the administration – should Trump figure out who they are. “The leak hunt is a big deal right now,” one administration official says. Another one of our sources says that they and others in the Trump-Vance administration were recently warned by a superior that (in the source’s paraphrase) “if you talked to Jonathan or Maggie [you] better be good at covering your tracks.”

On Tuesday, the Times ran an excerpt from the book that painted a stark portrait – with fly-on-the-wall levels of dialogue and detail – of pivotal, highly classified moments during Trump’s march to war with Iran. The excerpt provided a graphic account, pretty clearly derived from the knowledge of high-ranking officials working in the entrails of the Trump-Vance government, of what has been apparent since the start of the conflict.

Basically, nobody in Trump’s so-called war cabinet – save for “Donald the Dove” – thought the war was a slam-dunk great idea. But despite specific reservations voiced by self-professed war skeptics like JD Vance or hawks like Marco Rubio, they all adopted a go-along-to-get-along posture towards Trump’s warmongering. There are several moments in the excerpt that make Trump look, to quote one of his former secretaries of state, like a “fucking moron”: a doddering, self-embarassing fool bumbling his way into a bloodbath of his own construction.

These revelations appear to be just the start of an arterial gush of revelations that Swan and Haberman have obtained – and Trump was already pissed off, publicly fantasizing about suing the authors with billowing anger that predates this week’s Times story.

Last month, the US president erupted at Haberman online, posting that she’s “just another SLEAZEBAG writer for The Failing New York Times,” and saying, “I’m thinking of adding Maggot, and some of her ‘associates,’ into my Florida based Lawsuit against The Times.” He and his staff also attached a photo of Haberman to his post, making it clear where they wanted their Two Minutes Hate directed. At the time, his outburst seemed to come out of nowhere. But it was all about the book.

According to people familiar with the matter, Trump and his inner circle had been hearing whispers about embarrassing or damning information that was likely to appear in Haberman and Swan’s opus. (Full disclosure: Swan was one of my groomsmen… So if Trump puts him in a gulag for writing this Treasonous News Fakery, save me a seat in his cell, please. I’d like to come hang.) One close Trump adviser says the president started getting “a little nervous” about the book in March, and sporadically went on profanity-laced tirades declaring the leaks an invasion of his privacy and potentially “treason,” and demanding the names of senior appointees who anonymously leaked.

The sprawling leak hunt comes as the president is threatening to further escalate his already horrifying assault on free speech and a free press. It is also occurring as the Iran war tears apart the MAGA elite as nothing else has since Trump returned to power. And it’s happening as Trump has been privately venting that he can tell how some of his most senior officials, like Vance and Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard, have not been nearly as thrilled about the war as he would like.

The White House did not respond to Zeteo’s request for comment by the deadline. They were probably too busy, finger-pointing over which snitches need stitches.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s deputy health minister told Al Jazeera that at least 1,937 people had been killed in US-Israeli attacks. Iranian officials said last month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 1,530 people, including at least 130 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry. More than 200 people were killed on Wednesday alone.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 33 people, including 10 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Violations claim: Iranian Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf said ceasefire terms had been broken by Israeli strikes on Lebanon, a drone in Iranian airspace, and the White House denying Iran’s “right to enrichment.”

Gas prices: Crude oil prices dropped below $100 a barrel, but relief at the gas pump may be slow. Oil companies want the US to stop Iranian tolls on the Strait of Hormuz, saying they could add $2.5 million to the cost of each shipment.

‘REAL AGREEMENT’: Trump says troops sent to the region as part of the US military buildup will stay “until such time as the REAL AGREEMENT reached is fully complied with.” He added: “If for any reason it is not, which is highly unlikely, then the ‘Shootin’ Starts,’ bigger, and better, and stronger than anyone has ever seen before.”

Tariff threat: Donald Trump threatened to impose 50% tariffs on all goods imported from countries “supplying military weapons to Iran.”

Try again: Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer said he would introduce a new War Powers Resolution next week. He also slammed Trump because “Iran still has its nuclear stockpile, its nuclear ambitions are still unchecked if not accelerated [and] the nations of the world are furious.”

Reporters killed: Ghada Dayekh of Sawt-al-Farah and Suzan Khalil of Al-Manar TV and Al-Nour Radio were killed in Israeli strikes in Lebanon. The Committee to Protect Journalists said such deaths “should shock the conscience of the world.”

‘Such Carnage Defies Belief’

Vice President JD Vance claimed “a legitimate misunderstanding” meant “the Iranians thought the ceasefire included Lebanon, and it just didn’t” – even though Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, another key figure in talks, specifically said the truce did include Lebanon. As Vance twisted in the wind, Israeli forces carried on to horrific effect, slaughtering more than 200 people and injuring more than a thousand in civilian areas in Lebanon, on the deadliest day since the start of attacks. UN Human Rights Chief Volker Türk said: “Such carnage, within hours of agreeing to a ceasefire with Iran, defies belief.”

🗞️ What You Need to Know

Bondi bails: The DOJ said Pam Bondi would not be deposed by the House Oversight Committee about the Epstein affair on April 14, because she is no longer attorney general. Democrats and Republicans still want to depose her.

Pope nope: Leo XIV rejected a US visit after the Vatican’s US representative was harangued by a senior Pentagon official, multiple outlets reported. The Vatican is said to have detected a threat to use military force against it.

Pod save us: HHS Secretary and anti-vaccine crank Robert F Kennedy Jr announced a podcast, promising “fearless conversations” to “lift taboos” and “expose hypocrisy” and “lies” related to the nation’s health.

Chuck Chuck?: To retake the Senate, Minority Leader Chuck Schumer is ready to back progressives who want to remove him, TIME reported. Such Democratic candidates include Graham Platner in Maine and Mallory McMorrow in Michigan.

New York suit: Trump asked a court to toss a civil case in which he was ordered to pay a $500 million penalty for business fraud. That was already wiped, but other penalties still stand, and Trump sees AG Letitia James as a political enemy.

California scheming: Democrats celebrated after the state supreme court ordered sheriff and GOP gubernatorial candidate Chad Bianco to stop a “voter fraud” probe that saw him seize 650,000 ballots from last year’s special election.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

In 2024, Robert F Kennedy Jr admitted dumping a dead bear in Central Park 10 years before, to the mystification of New York police. Which disgraced actor heard Kennedy’s video confession?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israel killed Al Jazeera journalist Muhammad Washah and another Palestinian in an airstrike in Gaza City. He is the 12th Al Jazeera journalist killed since October 2023. Israel has killed more than 260 journalists in Gaza since the genocide began.

🇬🇷 Social media ban: Greek Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis banned social media for children under 15, starting next year. Australia passed a similar ban last year.

🇰🇷🇰🇵 Missiles report: South Korea said North Korea launched ballistic missiles towards the sea for a second time in two days, jeopardizing potential peace talks.

🇳🇬 Deadly village shooting: More than 20 people were reportedly killed by two gunmen in two villages in Nigeria’s Niger state, in an attack that lasted hours.

😵💫 WTF?!

In the face of genocidal threats against Iran from the US president, the first question US corporate media asks is how eliminating an entire foreign population will affect… investors. But of course.

🧠 Trivia answer: Roseanne Barr.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

🇬🇷 Social media ban: Greek Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis banned social media for children under 15, starting next year. Australia passed a similar ban last year.

🇰🇷🇰🇵 Missiles report: South Korea said North Korea launched ballistic missiles towards the sea for a second time in two days, jeopardizing potential peace talks.

🇳🇬 Deadly village shooting: More than 20 people were reportedly killed by two gunmen in two villages in Nigeria’s Niger state, in an attack that lasted hours.

😵💫 WTF?!

In the face of genocidal threats against Iran from the US president, the first question US corporate media asks is how eliminating an entire foreign population will affect… investors. But of course.

🧠 Trivia answer: Roseanne Barr.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

items.title: Unhinged Trump Threatens to Jail Reporters, Media Elite Can’t Wait to Party With Him
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we take issue with the establishment press delivering a collective shrug at the president’s increasingly deranged threats towards free speech – while they simultaneously prepare to “honor” him. Plus, we look into who Bari Weiss is wining and dining, and share the world’s worst history lesson delivered by Trump’s Fox brain trust.

Trump Ramps Up His War on the Press

Within the span of roughly 30 hours, Donald Trump threatened to jail journalists if they didn’t burn their confidential sources and accused a news network of criminal conduct for publishing an accurate public statement. And in both cases, he called for the feds to criminally investigate media outlets.

Yet, despite the media-bashing president ramping up his already white-hot incitement towards the fourth estate to new levels, the White House press corps tasked to cover him has delivered a collective shrug.

And all the while, the elite political media class is prepping to spend a night publicly feting this man during a lavish party. But don’t worry, because in an act of unparalleled bravery, they’ll make sure to show the president their defiant support for the First Amendment by wearing matching accessories.

During a Monday afternoon press conference, Trump raged about reports on a missing airman following the downing of an American jet fighter in Iran on Friday. Though the pilot was recovered within several hours, the “back-seater” was stranded until Sunday, when he was rescued during a daring mission.

“We’re looking very hard to find that leaker,” Trump fumed. “They didn’t know there was somebody missing until this leaker gave the information. So whoever it was, we think we’ll be able to find it out because we’re going to go to the media company that released it, and we’re going to say, national security, give it up or go to jail.”

Calling the source a “sick person,” Trump growled that a journalist at an unspecified news outlet that reported on the missing airman needed to give up their source’s name. “And the person that did the story will go to jail if he doesn’t say,” the president exclaimed. “They put this mission at great risk.”

While Trump didn’t say who he was referring to, and the White House merely said an “investigation is underway” when reached for comment, sources familiar with the situation tell my Zeteo colleague Asawin Suebsaeng that top Trump officials have identified leaks on this matter to CBS, Axios, and the New York Times as part of an investigation.

These sources confirmed that those were at least some of the news outlets Trump referred to when he called for unnamed reporters to be jailed if they don’t cough up their sources to him.

Israeli journalist Amit Segal – who has boasted of his proximity to Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu – identified himself as the first to report on the missing airman, though he somewhat backpedaled when approached by the New York Post. “I’m not sure I was the first,” he said. “And anyway — I will protect my sources.” Barak Ravid, Segal’s Channel 12 colleague who also works for Axios, was also among the earliest to report on the search for the crew member.

With Trump outright calling for the imprisonment of their brethren, one would think that the press corps on hand for the president’s briefing would at least follow up and question him on his latest media threat. Yet, despite taking questions for roughly 40 minutes, Trump was not once asked about it.

On Tuesday, however, a reporter did confront acting Attorney General Todd Blanche on the president’s remarks, asking if there is an ongoing leak investigation. He said he wouldn’t comment on current investigations other than to say “we will always investigate those types of leaks,” which “means sending a subpoena to the reporter.”

While the DC reporting class – now full of compliant MAGA lickspittles – largely shrugged off Trump’s “give it up or go to jail” threats, at least some press freedom organizations called the president out – while also noting a bit of irony.

“In fact, Trump’s hatred of the press runs so deep that, in his haste to lock up journalists for doing their jobs, he may have inadvertently threatened Israeli journalists and officials who were reportedly behind the story with prison,” Freedom of the Press Foundation chief of advocacy Seth Stern said. “Anyone who for some reason considers taking Trump seriously next time he goes on a half-baked anti-press rant should remember that.”

Soon after declaring he had reached a two-week ceasefire pact with Iran just 90 minutes before his 8pm genocidal deadline, Trump apparently revealed he was fully unaware of what the Iranian government was actually saying when he accused CNN of peddling fake news.

Claiming that an “alleged Statement put out” by CNN was a “FRAUD,” adding that he’d already shared Iran’s “Official” statement from its foreign minister, Trump demanded an immediate retraction and apology from the network while also calling for a criminal probe.

“Authorities are looking to determine whether or not a crime was committed on the issuance of the Fake CNN World Statement, or was it a sick rogue player? CNN is being ordered to immediately withdraw this Statement with full apologies for their, as usual, terrible ‘reporting,’” Trump added in his Truth Social post. “Results of the investigation will be announced in the near future.”

The White House’s social media rapid response team echoed the president’s rant, stating that the “alleged statement by ‘Iran’ is a FRAUD” and for CNN to “Correct immediately!” Joining the pile on, Brendan Carr – Trump’s appointed attack dog at the FCC who has no authority over cable news – called for “change at CNN” because they were “pushing out a hoax headline in such a sensitive national security moment.”

The problem is, the statement CNN shared was from the Islamic Republic’s Supreme National Security Council and had already appeared on Iran’s state news agencies. And, in fact, several other mainstream media outlets – such as the New York Times – had also reported on the statement.

Of course, the security council’s response boasted that Iran had achieved a “great victory” over the US and forced Trump to accept its 10-point peace plan, which would include Iran’s continued control over the Strait of Hormuz and all sanctions lifted – showing that Trump is already losing the messaging war.

“The statement in question was obtained by CNN from Iranian officials and reported on multiple Iranian state media outlets,” a CNN spokesperson said on Tuesday night. “We received the statement from specific official Iranian spokespeople who are known to us.”

The White House did not respond to a request for comment about the validity of CNN’s reporting.

Meanwhile, we are just a few weeks out from journalists and executives at many of these news outlets that Trump is looking to criminally punish attending a party where the press-hating president will be “honored” by the White House Correspondents’ Association.

But don’t worry, folks. Some of those reporters at the black-tie affair are going to wear pocket squares and pins emblazoned with the words of the First Amendment.

That’ll show him!

Bari Wines and Dines Pete

Speaking of the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, not only are the major networks and legacy news organizations participating this year despite Trump’s attendance and continued demonization of the press, but they are also bringing the president’s lackeys as guests.

Our friend Lachlan Cartwright at Breaker reported this week that a number of Trump administration officials and allies will have featured seats at the tables of prominent news outlets.

It should come as no surprise to our readers who CBS News boss Bari Weiss has asked to join her at the shindig: Pete Hegseth – the Fox morning weekend host turned Christian nationalist war chief, whose vendetta against press freedoms rivals even his boss’s.

While it’s not clear yet if Hegseth has accepted Weiss’s request to be her plus-one (the Pentagon and CBS haven’t returned my requests for comment), the invite shouldn’t come as too much of a shock.

As we reported last month, Weiss has booked Hegseth for on-air sitdowns, even as rival networks and publications remain largely unified in not pursuing interviews with the defense secretary in protest of his media policy changes at the Pentagon.

Meanwhile, it isn’t just conservative-leaning organizations – such as CBS or Rupert Murdoch’s media empire – that are rolling out the welcome wagon to Team Trump. Mainstream outlets like Reuters and ABC News have also extended invitations to Trump officials, according to Cartwright.

There is one media company that has announced it will not participate in this farcical charade. Earlier this week, HuffPost declared that it would not attend the dinner and would instead provide real-time coverage and analysis of the event for its readers.

“HuffPost remains committed to delivering rigorous, independent reporting that holds those in power accountable,” the outlet said in a statement.

With a Brain Trust Like This…

Despite the last-minute two-week ceasefire being an obvious major win for Iran and already in danger of being blown apart, it was no surprise that Trump’s sycophants at Fox would proclaim that he had hit a “home run” with the deal.

Still, the president’s ‘Fox News Cabinet,’ led by shadow chief of staff Sean Hannity and “The Great One” Mark Levin, continued to serve as Trump’s wartime consigliere through his TV on Tuesday night. Besides calling for the president to snatch all the enriched uranium from Iran – something Trump parroted on Wednesday morning – the Fox hosts also urged him to foment a civil war by arming the Iranian population.

And the historic examples they used to support their case were beyond parody.

“We have to arm the people... Reagan did it in Afghanistan. He did it in Nicaragua. He did it in Honduras. He did it in Angola. Donald Trump can do exactly the same thing,” Levin exclaimed on Tuesday night.

“I think with the freedom fighters against the Contras, it worked. For the Mujahideen in Afghanistan, it helped beat the Soviet Union. I think it would be very effective,” Hannity responded.

Setting aside that apparently Hannity doesn’t realize that the Contras were the supposed “freedom fighters” in 1980s Nicaragua, do we really need to point out just how ridiculous these comparisons are? And how that all worked out in the long run?

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

items.title: I Watched Right-Wing Journalists Try to Shame Abdul El-Sayed and Hasan Piker
items.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
items.content:
Hundreds of Michiganders waited in long lines in the cold for Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed’s rallies with Hasan Piker at two universities.

Zeteo was inside those rallies, where the national reporters who traveled to Michigan for the events seemed extremely puzzled as to why the progressive politician would choose to campaign with the popular leftist Twitch streamer – and they were intently focused on Israel.

“Hasan, do you feel like your presence on the campaign trail may hurt the candidates that you’re supporting more than help them?” a Fox News reporter asked. Another reporter asked El-Sayed what he would say to people who attend a local synagogue that was the site of a foiled terrorist attack, and are “concerned now about you campaigning with Hasan.”

Olivia Reingold, a writer for Bari Weiss’s Free Press who has defended Israel’s manufactured famine in Gaza, created a viral moment for El-Sayed when she asked him, “Does Israel have a right to exist as a Jewish state?”

“What do you mean by Jewish state?” El-Sayed responded. As Reingold stumbled and failed to answer, he retorted: “If you can’t define the question, I’m not going to answer your question.”

In recent weeks, a well-funded centrist think tank and El-Sayed’s Democratic opponents have led a campaign to try to publicly cancel Piker, and El-Sayed for rallying with him. And as President Donald Trump was actively threatening to commit genocide in Iran on Tuesday, the US media was busy attempting to shame El-Sayed over some of Piker’s past controversial comments. Perhaps unsurprisingly, the journalists filed stories with headlines focused on the Muslim men’s anti-Israel views and Piker’s old remarks.

“Michigan Senate candidate El-Sayed declines to disavow Hasan Piker’s past comments,” Politico wrote. Jewish Insider’s headline went further: “Abdul El-Sayed emphasizes anti-Israel rhetoric at rallies with antisemitic streamer Hasan Piker.” The Jewish Telegraphic Agency headlined its piece: “In rallies taking on Israel, a defiant Hasan Piker boosts Michigan Senate candidate Abdul el-Sayed.”

You would never know from the reports that El-Sayed’s rallies were, in fact, focused around affordability, getting money out of politics and into people’s pockets, and securing Medicare for All. Nor would you know that El-Sayed’s decision to invite Piker to his rallies at the University of Michigan and Michigan State University has been a big boon for his campaign.

El-Sayed’s communications director, Roxie Richner, told Zeteo that the campaign has seen an “increase in momentum” over the last month. It now has 4,000 volunteers, and the El-Sayed campaign rose to one the top five fundraisers on ActBlue the week after the Piker events were announced. Richner said over 1,000 people signed up for each rally, and both halls, which had over 500 seats, were filled to capacity.

‘I Go Everywhere’

El-Sayed is one of three Democrats running in a hotly contested primary that could determine the balance of power in the Senate. El-Sayed is the most progressive candidate in the primary – he is the only one in the race who has never taken corporate PAC cash, supports abolishing ICE, and champions Medicare for All. He has also been the most outspoken opponent of Israel’s genocide, and has vowed not to send any arms to Israel, including so-called defensive weapons. The genocide likely cost Democrats Michigan in 2024, a key swing state that is home to a sizeable Arab and Muslim population.

It makes sense why he would campaign with Piker. The 34-year-old Twitch streamer represents the left flank in the streaming space that is traditionally dominated by the right. Piker has amassed an audience of over 3 million followers, many of them young men, a key electorate that flocked to Trump in 2024.

On Tuesday, Piker’s nearly 10-hour stream of El-Sayed’s rallies and his day on the campaign trail drew over 1 million viewers.

Third Way, a billionaire-funded think tank, has spearheaded the cancellation campaign, and pressured El-Sayed to uninvite Piker from Tuesday’s rally. It must be noted the group has vowed to be the “chief opponent of the left” in the 2028 Democratic presidential primary and plans to spend $30 million to $50 million on this effort. Third Way has said it opposes candidates who support Medicare for All, the Green New Deal, abolishing ICE, or “open borders.”

Some establishment Democrats in Congress joined the chorus, with some pledging to Politico that they would never join Piker’s stream. Senator Cory Booker, one of the Democrats preemptively refusing to go on Piker’s show, claimed on ‘Pod Save America’ that he’d never actually heard of the streamer before the recent news cycle.

El-Sayed’s primary opponents, AIPAC-backed Rep. Haley Stevens and state Senator Mallory McMorrow, have also slammed El-Sayed for campaigning with Piker. McMorrow absurdly compared him to neo-Nazi streamer Nick Fuentes, citing controversial comments Piker made in the wake of the Hamas-led attacks on Israel and about Israel’s genocide in Gaza.

Speaking about his opponents, El-Sayed told Zeteo on Tuesday night, “They have a vision of the people they want to be talking to. Mine is a lot more expansive. I go everywhere and talk to everybody.”

In recent weeks, Piker has come under fire for having said during a 2019 livestream that “America deserved 9/11.” He later apologized for the comment and contended that he was referring to the concept of blowback, where US global intervention can lead to hostility from foreign groups.

But the main criticism levied against Piker is antisemitism. Piker once called Orthodox Jews in Israel “inbred.” He recently expressed regret for the comment, while noting he has also used that term as a general pejorative to describe neo-Nazis, white supremacists, and other groups.

Piker has defended himself as a fervent, consistent opponent of antisemitism and far-right fascism. El-Sayed has repeatedly said in recent weeks that he does not necessarily agree with everything the streamer has said but wants to connect with his audience and meet voters where they are.

‘Manufactured Controversy’

At both events, it was clear the cancellation campaign against Piker has failed.

While the national reporters filed stories about El-Sayed’s rhetoric on Israel and Piker’s past controversial comments, MSU’s student newspaper reported that most of the students they interviewed had attended the event because Piker was invited.

Some attendees similarly told Zeteo that Piker’s appearance was why they decided to come to a political rally on a Tuesday afternoon, and were willing to wait in long lines.

“Manufactured controversy” is how one attendee, Nick Coffin-Callis, described the recent anti-Piker campaign to Zeteo at Michigan State University ahead of the rally.

Standing outside to get into the rally at the University of Michigan, Dustin Krcatovich, an El-Sayed supporter, said that the Piker discourse was “preposterous but expected.”

“If somebody is reaching people to the degree that he is, people are going to try to shut him down if they’re in opposition of what he represents,” Krcatovich said. “I think if you listen to something that’s anything more than a 15-second soundbite… it’s pretty clear where his morals and ethics are.”

At the Ann Arbor campus, the line to get into the hall wrapped around the building, and people were eventually directed to an overflow area once it filled up.

El-Sayed, who has not bowed to pressure to sever ties with the streamer, told Zeteo on Tuesday night at the University of Michigan that his strategy to connect with young men in this campaign is to “go where they are,” while presenting a “benevolent” version of masculinity that counters the toxicity perpetuated by MAGA figureheads.

While Tuesday marked Piker’s first time stumping for a candidate, he’s been involved in political commentary for years, and Democrats have appeared on his stream. Piker was even invited to stream from the Democratic National Convention in 2024.

When Zeteo asked Piker about why Democrats have chosen this moment to fight about him, he said “they would rather talk about anything but the thing that many of them wanted, which is this war.”

But he said in the age of new media, voices like his can no longer be gatekept or cast aside, pointing to the “sea change” on Palestine.

Piker said the party’s “manufactured outrage” over him is rooted in fear, because establishment Democrats are losing their hold over the party, especially as public opinion shifts further away from them on key issues like abolishing ICE, stopping Israel’s genocide, and opposing the war in Iran.

“They are united in their hatred of not just me, but all of us, and I welcome their hatred,” Piker told the Ann Arbor crowd.

After the rally, Piker told Zeteo he’s supporting El-Sayed’s campaign because he is “exactly the change that I want to see in the Democratic Party, a party that is actually going to fight back against the excesses and the violence that the fascist Republicans represent.”

Other prominent figures on the left, including Senator Bernie Sanders, have refused to distance themselves from Piker.

Progressive Michigan Rep. Rashida Tlaib – a surprise guest at the Ann Arbor rally – took to the stage and told Piker he was “never going to get canceled up in Michigan.”

Tlaib told the crowd that El-Sayed is threatening to some because he represents a movement of people that are “sick and tired” of sellouts in Congress. “And let me tell you – all the haters can keep hating, because when I ran, I only won by 900 votes, and now they can’t get rid of me,” she said.

Rep. Summer Lee, who defeated an AIPAC-backed challenger in 2024 after criticizing Israel, drove down from her district in Pittsburgh to support El-Sayed alongside Piker.

“There’s a lot of people who did not want us to be here with y’all today, it was folks who don’t want us talking to people who might rock the boat,” she told a full house at the University of Michigan.

Speaking with Zeteo about the anti-Piker discourse, Lee said there are “concerted efforts to distract us from our attempts at building solidarity.”

When Zeteo asked El-Sayed why Democrats have chosen this moment to fight about Piker, he responded with a smile: “I’ll leave that to readers to surmise for themselves.”

Have a tip for Minnah? Reach out on Signal at minnahfarshad.94.

items.title: 'No Doubt War Will Resume': Skeptical Iranians Say It's Too Early to Celebrate
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
TEHRAN, Iran – The morning after US President Donald Trump announced a two-week ceasefire with Iran, the underlying mood here in Tehran was far from relief.

As people continued their daily routines, as they had in the days before, a divide emerged in how Iranians were reacting to the US president’s announcement. Many saw the ceasefire as a victory for Iran, while some thought their country had agreed to it too soon. Others remained deeply distrustful of the Americans, and many expressed an instinctive determination to keep pressure on both Washington and the country’s own leadership. There was, however, an overall unifying feeling of raw defiance among the Iranians I spoke to.

Ahmad, a 42-year-old bookseller, saw the pause as a sign of Trump’s humiliation, suggesting the failure to secure the Strait of Hormuz might cause Gulf states to reconsider supporting America.

Saeed, a 65-year-old taxi driver, viewed the ceasefire as a victory, comparing Trump’s actions to Saddam Hussein’s invasion in 1980, which led to a nearly eight-year war between the two countries. “They said Iran would surrender in three days,” Saeed exclaimed. “But now the Strait of Hormuz is in our hands.

Negin, a 27-year-old barista, argued the ceasefire came too soon, referring to the deaths of scores of schoolchildren in Minab. “After so much innocent blood, there should not have been a ceasefire this quickly,” she told me.

“I have no doubt the war will resume,” she added. “The US and Israel cannot be trusted.”

Meanwhile, Sobhan, a 56-year-old restaurant cleaner, believed the ceasefire was merely a prelude to renewed unrest and insisted people must maintain their presence in public spaces.

The tense morning could not be separated from the night that preceded it.

As the deadline for Trump’s threat to wipe out “a whole civilization” approached, some residents had prepared by storing water and charging power banks, while others attempted to leave the cities. But a significant number took a different path: they poured into the streets.

In cities including Ahvaz, capital of the southwestern province of Khuzestan, and Tehran, citizens formed human chains to protect the infrastructure that Trump was threatening to blow up. On Ahvaz’s White Bridge, hundreds linked hands across the Karun River in an act of defiance. Similar scenes unfolded near power stations and public squares in the capital. Participants described the gatherings not as spontaneous protests, but as deliberate attempts to project unity and send a message that the country would not be intimidated.

In the final hours leading up to Trump’s deadline, Tehran did not resemble a city on the brink of war. By 10pm – five and a half hours before the deadline – shops remained open. Large groups filled the streets in car convoys and on foot, planning to remain outside together until the 3:30am deadline passed.

Marzieh, a 35-year-old philosophy graduate, brought water, tea, and snacks to sit with her friends. “I feel safer here,” she said. “In the square, alongside my friends and the people of my country.” Her husband Kazem, a civil engineer, echoed the sentiment. “Whatever happens, I want to experience it alongside my people. These are moments you cannot miss.”

Enghelab Square in central Tehran, historically the focal point for major gatherings, was heavily crowded. Sara, 38, who recently returned after 15 years in Canada, told her friends she could not accept the humiliation of an ancient civilization being threatened by a newly established country. “Paying attention to Trump is itself a form of surrender,” she said. “The best response is to ignore his nonsense altogether.”

Around midnight, the sound of air defense systems and distant fighter jets echoed, but the crowd quickly drowned out the noise with chants of “Allahu Akbar.” Ali and Pourandokht, a couple aged 50 and 57, told me they had made no emergency preparations. Pointing out that electricity in their area had been targeted previously but restored within hours, they expressed confidence in the country’s institutions. “Taking him [Trump] seriously is the biggest mistake,” they said. “He is a deceitful opportunist.”

Sahar Daneshvar, a 40-year-old journalist and television presenter, stayed in Enghelab Square until 6:00am. She noted that crowds were significantly larger than on previous nights as people anticipated what Trump might do at the 3:30am mark.

As the deadline approached, “murmurs began to ripple through the crowd,” Daneshvar recalled. Sitting near two individuals discussing an unconfirmed ceasefire, she noticed the idea unsettled them. They questioned the timing and purpose of the pause. Once Trump’s post appeared, it became the focal point of the crowd. Daneshvar argued with a friend who dismissed the statement as an empty claim; she countered that his tone was noticeably different this time.

Minutes later, news networks ran breaking news alerts, followed by an official statement from the Supreme National Security Council. A dominant consensus then emerged across the discussion circles: they were not leaving the square. “Just as we stood alongside our military forces until now, we will stand alongside our diplomats to ensure that the conditions set are fulfilled,” many argued, according to Daneshvar.

Sara Aghili, a 29-year-old political science graduate, offered a broader interpretation of the night. “If there is a confrontation with an external enemy, it is not only the military that is engaged,” she said. “People see their presence, holding flags, gathering in the streets, as a form of participation in that struggle.”

It’s difficult to gauge where all Iranians stand on the ceasefire, as many who are anti-government are too nervous to speak to reporters. Iran and Washington appeared to have different interpretations of what was agreed to as a basis for negotiations. By Wednesday afternoon, reports of a massive Israeli attack on Lebanon began circulating, raising the probability of renewed escalation. It was later reported that Iran had again closed the Strait of Hormuz in response to Israel’s attacks on Lebanon.

Here in Tehran, Mohammadreza, a 39-year-old mechanic, adopted a cautious stance. “We have to wait and see what happens,” he said. “For now, people should stay in the streets. It’s too early to make any final judgment.”

Hanieh Qasemian is an Iranian journalist based between Iran and Lebanon. She also studies Palestinian Studies at the University of Tehran. Her work focuses on Middle Eastern affairs, militant groups, and political Islam.

Editor’s note: This article was published in collaboration with Egab. For those who asked Zeteo to protect their identity so they could speak freely, only first names were included.
