items.title: 🇮🇷 🇺🇸 Did Iran Just Win This War?
items.author: First Draft | Zeteo
items.content:
On this day in 2018, Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán won the general election by a landslide, securing his third consecutive term in office. The far-right authoritarian is up for reelection once again, and despite receiving support from Donald Trump and JD Vance, who traveled to Budapest this week, Orbán’s chances aren’t looking as promising this time around.

Good morning! Andrew, Swin, and Prem here, with some decent news. Donald Trump opted not to try to slaughter Iran’s “whole civilization” Tuesday evening, as he had threatened. In Trump’s disturbing second term, we must take wins where we can. Who knows how long the “ceasefire” will last, but we, of course, welcome it: No more civilians should have to die just because our president is a raging idiot, sociopath, and pawn of Benjamin Netanyahu.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ the ceasefire framework appears to be “a strategic win” for Iran, and a major loss for Trump and Netanyahu; liberals expand their majority on Wisconsin’s Supreme Court; and Janet Mills goes dark on the air with two months left in Maine’s Senate primary.

Don Backs Down

Donald Trump on Tuesday threatened to destroy Iran’s “whole civilization” unless its leaders agreed to a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

By Tuesday evening – instead of trying to kill the 90 million people living in Iran, or bombing Iran’s power plants and bridges as he had promised – Trump announced “a double sided CEASEFIRE!” for two weeks. In doing so, Trump all but admitted the reality that his illegal war is going terribly – and Iran is winning.

“We received a 10-point proposal from Iran, and believe it is a workable basis on which to negotiate,” the president announced on Truth Social – apparently referring to the 10-point plan that Iran had presented to the US before he threatened to murder its entire population.

Using this 10-point plan as a framework for a deal, rather than the previous American 15-point plan, is obviously a major win for Iran – which is, indeed, declaring victory. Experts and commentators see it that way, too.

“If Iran did in fact secure guarantees based on the ‘10 principles’ Trump referenced, that is not a marginal outcome, it is a strategic win for Tehran, reinforcing its narrative and positioning,” wrote Danny Citrinowicz, a former top Israeli intelligence officer who focused on Iran. As former US State Department official and veteran Middle East negotiator Aaron David Miller put it, “Iran has won another round.”

Political scientist Robert A. Pape writes: “Huge strategic defeat for the US, biggest loss since Vietnam.”

The 10-point plan would allow Iran to continue its control over the Strait of Hormuz, the crucial waterway that Iran has closed since the start of the war, stopping the flow of oil and throttling the global economy.

Just a day earlier, Trump was fantasizing about the US “charging tolls” for ships to transit the strait. Now, it looks much more likely that Iran will keep doing this instead, and that it will get to charge “a $2M fee per ship.” In another win for Iran, the plan would involve lifting “all US sanctions on Iran.”

Some liberals will invariably revel in “TACO Trump” memes about how Trump always chickens out. But, as we reported at Zeteo on Tuesday, none of this is over. The ceasefire, as of now, is only temporary. As Trump advisers and others in the upper tiers of Trumplandia warned us, there’s still a decent chance that he escalates the war again soon.

Here’s why.

“A problem is Donald Trump hates being humiliated,” one senior administration official told Swin on Tuesday. “He is going to see coverage that he was beat by the Iranians. I am expecting that to change his thinking.”

This official – who thinks the war is dragging down this presidency – also told Swin that they are trying to get a bunch of “positive” media coverage of Trump’s latest move placed in front of the president in the coming days, in order to soothe his ego and maybe (just maybe) halt an Iran-war apocalypse. This source said they are also going to talk to their contacts in right-wing media and MAGA-influencer spheres to see what kind of content those media figures are generating on Trump’s alleged “win.”

Yet, the idea that Trump “won” will be hard to support – given that he, after threatening to wipe out Iran, apparently accepted Iran’s proposal as a framework for negotiating an end to the war.

And nothing about this war can be construed as a win for the US. The war itself shouldn’t be happening. It began with a heinous war crime that killed more than 150 schoolgirls; saw the US attack a defenseless, uninvolved Iranian naval ship; and has featured numerous strikes on Iran’s civilian infrastructure (with Iran targeting civilian infrastructure around the region, in kind).

At least 13 US soldiers have died, and hundreds have been injured. Over 1,900 Iranians have been killed. And people all around the world are paying significantly higher energy prices due to this reckless war, which is expected to cause food shortages and fuel shortages. The war will likely make essential molecules scarce, which could cause another supply-chain crisis like the one we saw from COVID-19… or worse.

The attack on Iran was based on lies, it was completely unnecessary, and no one wanted it – other than Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, who reportedly sold the US president on a rosy and deeply misguided vision of how the war would play out, per a deep dive from the New York Times on Tuesday. (Netanyahu got to brief Trump in the White House Situation Room, and Trump opted not to sit at the head of the table as he did so.)

If Tuesday night’s ceasefire was an admission of defeat from Trump, it was a much bigger loss for Netanyahu.

According to Israeli journalist Suleiman Maswadeh, Israel was “surprised” by Trump’s ceasefire announcement Tuesday night, with a source telling him: “We received updates in the final moments when everything seemed finalized. We are committed to the ceasefire.”

As Israeli analyst Mairav Zonszein noted, if the war ends this way, it will appear exceptionally pointless to Israeli civilians: “For now it looks like Israelis have been in shelters for 5 weeks in order to reopen Strait of Hormuz that was open before the war and to degrade some capabilities that can be built up within weeks.”

The 10-point framework reportedly includes Lebanon, where Israel has been leading another vicious ethnic-cleansing and mass-displacement campaign… though Israel says it won’t stop attacking the country. Iran continued to launch attacks on Israel after the tentative ceasefire was announced. Kuwait and the UAE also reported intercepting Iranian drones shortly after Trump’s announcement. Iranian state media, meanwhile, said an oil refinery on the country’s Lavan Island was struck hours after the news of the ceasefire.

We don’t know yet whether this ceasefire will last, and how or whether the war in Iran will end. At least Trump backed down, for now.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s deputy health minister told Al Jazeera that at least 1,937 people had been killed in US-Israeli attacks. Iranian officials said last month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 1,530 people, including at least 130 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 33 people, including 10 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Vance’s moment: Vice President JD Vance, who was reportedly brought in late Tuesday to help facilitate the 11th-hour agreement, called the ceasefire “a fragile truce.” He said some Iranian officials were misrepresenting the agreement, adding that while Trump told his negotiating team to come to the table, “if the Iranians don’t do the exact same thing, they’re going to find out that the president of the United States is not one to mess around.”

Oil prices plunge: The price of Brent crude plunged to $95 a barrel on Wednesday, a 13% decrease from before the ceasefire was announced. Though as the New York Times noted, the price per barrel is still around 30% higher than it was before Trump launched the war.

Kharg Island struck: Ahead of Trump’s ceasefire announcement, the US unleashed a new wave of strikes on Kharg Island, a five-mile-long coral outcrop in the Persian Gulf that supports roughly 90% of Iran’s crude oil exports.

Synagogue ‘completely destroyed’: US-Israeli strikes “completely destroyed” the Rafi-Nia Synagogue in Tehran yesterday, during Passover. “Our Torah scrolls were left under the rubble,” a Jewish representative in the country’s Islamic Consultative Assembly said.

Lebanon latest: After saying the two-week ceasefire would not extend to Lebanon, Israel hit the southern city of Tyre, while announcing new “evacuation” orders for several neighborhoods of Beirut.

American journalist freed: After a week in captivity, American journalist Shelly Kittleson was released yesterday by Iran-backed paramilitary group Kata’ib Hezbollah. In a statement, the group said that she would be released “on the condition that she leaves the country immediately.”

Bad-Faith Bari Buddy Battered

Michigan Democratic primary Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed absolutely destroyed Bari Weiss employee and bad-faith Free Press reporter Olivia Reingold, after she asked him whether he supports Israel’s “right to exist as a Jewish state.”

💬 Quote Unquote

“It’s bullshit.”

That’s apparently how even the hawkish Secretary of State Marco Rubio described the Iran regime change scenarios that Benjamin Netanyahu pitched to Donald Trump back in mid-February.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

Another ICE shooting: ICE shot and injured a man in Northern California yesterday. Acting ICE Director Todd Lyons claimed, without providing evidence, that the man was a gang member wanted in El Salvador. It comes just a day after security footage from a separate ICE shooting in Minneapolis revealed that officers lied under oath.

Replacing MTG: Trump-backed candidate Clay Fuller came out victorious over Democrat Shawn Harris in the runoff election to succeed former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene. While the GOP may retain this seat, the race still showed a major overperformance for Democrats.

Blue wave rising: Liberals will now hold a 5-2 majority on the Wisconsin Supreme Court, after Chris Taylor won an easy victory last night.

Same old DHS: New Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin is threatening to halt customs processing for international travelers at major US airports in “sanctuary cities” like New York and Los Angeles. The move could block nearly all international travel to these airports just months ahead of the this year’s World Cup.

Maine latest: With two months left in the Senate primary, Governor Janet Mills doesn’t have any more ads booked to run on broadcast TV. As oyster farmer Graham Platner pivots to the general election, he’ll start running this new ad featuring an endorsement from a lobsterman who’s voted for Susan Collins.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

How many members of Congress called for Trump to be impeached or removed from office via the 25th Amendment yesterday?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israel provided cover as the Israeli-backed Abu Nasira militia launched an attack on the Maghazi refugee camp yesterday, Drop Site News reported.

🇵🇸 Beaten to death: Sixty-eight-year-old Sabriya Shamasneh was beaten to death by Israeli soldiers who raided her family home in Jayyous during the early hours of Tuesday morning. It is the latest incident of escalating Israeli violence in the occupied West Bank.

🇹🇷🇮🇱 Attack outside Israeli consulate: Gunmen opened fire near the building housing the Israeli consulate in Istanbul, leading to a gunfight with security services. The three gunmen were killed in what both Israel and Turkey declared a “terror” attack.

🇦🇺🇦🇫 Veteran charged with war crimes: Ben Roberts-Smith, one of Australia’s most decorated living veterans, was charged with five counts of war crime murder for allegedly killing five unarmed people while serving in Afghanistan from 2009 to 2012.

🇻🇳 Communist Party chief elected as president: Vietnam’s National Assembly unanimously elected Communist Party Secretary General To Lam to a five-year term as president. Many critics view the move as a dangerous step toward authoritarianism, as Lam has now consolidated his control over both the party and the country.

📊 Chart of the Day

Even when 60% of Americans disapprove of Israel, the majority of our lawmakers will still likely continue to send them billions of dollars year after year.

(See the full polling results from Pew here.)

🧠 Trivia answer: At least 86 members of Congress.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Melanie Riehl and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

In Trump’s disturbing second term, we must take wins where we can. Who knows how long the “ceasefire” will last, but we, of course, welcome it: No more civilians should have to die just because our president is a raging idiot, sociopath, and pawn of Benjamin Netanyahu.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ the ceasefire framework appears to be “a strategic win” for Iran, and a major loss for Trump and Netanyahu; liberals expand their majority on Wisconsin’s Supreme Court; and Janet Mills goes dark on the air with two months left in Maine’s Senate primary.

Donald Trump on Tuesday threatened to destroy Iran’s “whole civilization” unless its leaders agreed to a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

“We received a 10-point proposal from Iran, and believe it is a workable basis on which to negotiate,” the president announced on Truth Social – apparently referring to the 10-point plan that Iran had presented to the US before he threatened to murder its entire population.

As former US State Department official and veteran Middle East negotiator Aaron David Miller put it, “Iran has won another round.”

Political scientist Robert A. Pape writes: “Huge strategic defeat for the US, biggest loss since Vietnam.”

The 10-point plan would allow Iran to continue its control over the Strait of Hormuz, the crucial waterway that Iran has closed since the start of the war, stopping the flow of oil and throttling the global economy.

Just a day earlier, Trump was fantasizing about the US “charging tolls” for ships to transit the strait. Now, it looks much more likely that Iran will keep doing this instead, and that it will get to charge “a $2M fee per ship.” In another win for Iran, the plan would involve lifting “all US sanctions on Iran.”

items.title: BREAKING: Trump Begs Off Pledge to Genocide 90 Million
items.author: Zeteo
items.content:
Don’t Miss It:

Mehdi and special guests will host a LIVE discussion at 8pm ET tonight (5pm PT; 1am BST) to discuss Trump’s walkback and the latest from Iran.

Donald Trump has tapped the brakes on his menacing threat to kill Iran’s “whole civilization.”

In a Truth Social post just over an hour before his 8:00pm deadline, Trump wrote that, based on conversations with the Pakistani government and supposed progress on a deal with Iran, he has agreed to “suspend the bombing and attack of Iran for a period of two weeks,” saying “it will be a double sided CEASEFIRE!”

Trump said the reason for his walkback was that “we have already met and exceeded all Military objectives and are very far along” with a deal.

He said the US received a 10-point proposal from Iran, and says “it is a workable basis on which to negotiate,” adding that the two-week ceasefire is subject to Iran agreeing “to the COMPLETE, IMMEDIATE, and SAFE OPENING of the Strait of Hormuz.”

It’s unclear if the 10-point proposal is the same one that Iran had already presented – before Trump threatened to destroy Iran’s “whole civilization.”

Iran’s Supreme National Security Council said it accepted the ceasefire but with a warning: “Our hands remain upon the trigger, and should the slightest error be committed by the enemy, it shall be met with full force.” Iran’s foreign minister said Tehran will allow passage through the Strait of Hormuz under Iranian military supervision.

Israel has reportedly agreed to the ceasefire as well.

Trump administration officials and others close to the White House caution Zeteo that we aren’t out of the woods yet – not by a mile. During this war, the US president has careened so quickly between war plans, ordering escalation and threatening mass murder, and then suddenly racing back to his desire to save face and to declare immediate “victory,” and then flopping back in the opposite direction again. By early afternoon in Washington on Tuesday, various Trump advisers and administration officials toldZeteo that chances were sky-high that Trump would dramatically escalate soon. Some sources left the door open for a scenario where the president takes his foot off the genocidal gas on Tuesday, only to rev the war-crime machine up again in weeks, if not days. None of the sources viewed Tuesday’s deadline as a mere negotiation tactic.

On Tuesday night, two close Trump advisers say they are still expecting Trump to escalate this war in the future. Each did, however, breathe a sigh of relief that the apocalyptic war crimes were put on hold. There are few in Trump’s inner orbit who thought this war would be a good idea, or who think it’s currently anything other than a political disaster for Team Trump.

Those senior officials, however, will continue to go along to get along with whatever the Mad King decrees.

Check out more from Zeteo:

items.title: Trump’s New Red Scare Is Coming to State and Local Police Near You
items.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
items.content:
Don’t Miss It:

Mehdi and special guests will host a LIVE discussion at 8pm ET tonight (5pm PT; 1am BST) to discuss Trump’s “deadline” and the latest from Iran.

FBI emails obtained by Zeteo show that the Trump administration is pushing state and local police to target Americans with left-wing views, emails show. As Donald Trump’s hot war in Iran enters its second month with no end in sight, a quieter, colder war is simmering on the home front.

Its target is not a foreign power thousands of miles away, but US citizens who disagree with the president and his hardline advisers. This domestic war’s chief weapon is a national security presidential memorandum, NSPM-7, which attempts to criminalize a laundry list of common left-wing beliefs and viewpoints.

Trump’s FBI is conducting trainings for state and local law enforcement officials on the president’s NSPM-7 directive, in order to help enlist them in the administration’s ongoing crackdown on the left, emails show. And it’s happening at the same time as Trump’s war in Iran is throttling energy prices and the global economy – creating even more discontent with his administration.

What this development means in practice is if you have criticized a corporation on social media, held a sign that demands ICE leave your city, shared a meme about alleged UnitedHealthcare CEO killer Luigi Mangione, or publicly condemned Trump’s Iran war at a local town hall, both the federal government and your local police could take note and forward along a list of your thoughtcrimes to the FBI for further review.

Unlike a standard-issue presidential directive that nudges a sector of the federal bureaucracy or realigns a federal agency towards the president’s liking, national security directives target the entire intelligence and defense apparatus, which is why they are almost always classified. NSPM-7, which was published publicly by the White House in September, makes no effort to conceal its goals.

The presidential directive is the most significant shift in domestic counter-terrorism efforts since the Patriot Act was introduced into law in the wake of 9/11. The program is sweeping in its scope, not only for the list of subversive activities it seeks to target, but also in the joint terrorism task forces it relies upon.

In an effort to confront “domestic terrorism and organized political violence,” NSPM-7 lists the following ideological indicators as targets for surveillance and counter-terror enforcement: antifascism; anti-Americanism; anti-capitalism; anti-Christianity; support for the overthrow of the United States government; what it calls extremism on migration, race, and gender; and hostility toward those who hold traditional American views on family, religion, and morality.

Each of these terms gives law enforcement an incentive to surveil and investigate anyone even approximating the ideology in the above list. Criticizing US foreign policy, condemning neo-Nazi violence, speaking out against racial and gender inequality, or even disagreeing with the president are now bases for law enforcement investigations.

Emails obtained by Zeteo show Trump’s new red scare is not confined to the Department of Justice and FBI – it is also being injected into local and state police forces across the country.

“Existing laws give the president great latitude in combating threats to national security,” Faiza Patel, the Brennan Center’s Senior director for Liberty and National Security wrote in an expert brief on the roll out of Trump’s national security directive, “NSPM-7 and the antifa executive order are an obvious abuse of this latitude, threatening to turn the full force of the federal government to rooting out a conjured-up left-wing conspiracy of political violence funded by shadowy figures… we will all be less safe as law enforcement resources are diverted from real threats to imagined ones.”

“The president’s [NPSM-7] memorandum is focused on investigating, disrupting, dismantling, and prosecuting individuals and entities engaged in organized political violence and domestic terrorism – specifically the sophisticated and organized campaigns that necessitate a new law enforcement strategy,” White House spokeswoman Abigail Jackson tells Zeteo, adding: “Left-wing organizations have fueled violent riots, organized attacks against law enforcement officers, coordinated illegal doxing campaigns, arranged drop points for weapons and riot materials, and more.”

Training Police on the New Red Scare

At the beginning of March, the FBI’s Office of Partner Engagement sent out an email to the Homeland Security and Emergency Management Agency in Washington, DC. This agency houses one of the country’s fusion centers, which act as intelligence-sharing hubs between federal, state, and local law enforcement. (There are over 75 fusion centers in the US.)

“Presenters from the FBI’s Counterterrorism Division... will provide an overview of NSPM-7, which establishes a US government strategy to counter domestic terrorism and organized political violence and outlines measures to investigate and disrupt extremist movement,” reads the email, which was sent days after Trump launched his war in Iran.

According to a former fusion center director who spoke with Zeteo on the condition of anonymity, this NSPM-7 training will likely be replicated in the vast majority of fusion centers covering the contiguous continental United States, in addition to Alaska, Guam, and Hawaii. Those fusion centers will then pass NSPM-7 directives to the state and local police departments they include as partners.

The FBI email was sent by Leo Schmitz, the assistant director for the FBI’s Office of Partner Engagement. According to his Linkedin profile, Schmitz is the former chief of police at the Cook County Sheriff’s Department in Illinois, one of the largest sheriff’s offices in America.

According to Schmitz’s email, the FBI’s “law enforcement partner speaker series” provides insight into “operational activities, intelligence, emerging trends, and best practices.” In other words, it appears that the FBI is instructing police on who they are targeting, what they know, where police should go looking for more information, and how to carry out local investigations based on NSPM-7.

This type of explicit instruction was presaged in a December memo from (recently fired) Attorney General Pam Bondi to implement the president’s national security directive targeting Americans who oppose Trump’s MAGA movement.

“Federal law enforcement will prioritize this threat,” Bondi wrote. “Where federal crime is encountered, federal agents will act. And where domestic terrorism is encountered or suspected, law enforcement agencies shall refer such matters to the Joint Terrorism Task Forces (JTTFs) for the exhaustive investigation contemplated by NSPM-7.”

While technically predating 9/11 in limited scope, the FBI’s joint terrorism task forces proliferated after the terrorist attack and created an information sharing environment that combined FBI personnel with those from other federal agencies including the DHS, DEA, and at times even the CIA and NSA. The joint terrorism task forces also include members of local and state police, creating a vertically integrated law enforcement network.

The power of joint terrorism task forces to federalize local and state police has long been cause for concern. In New York City, civil liberties advocates have warned of the risks posed by NYPD officers being deputized into the FBI through the New York joint terrorism task force, and representing themselves as federal law enforcement during enforcement actions.

Those concerns have risen to a new high as documents obtained by a non-profit, Property of the People, show dozens of NSPM-7-related investigations at joint terrorism task forces across the country.

Criminalizing Dissent

The Bondi memo, as it is known, instructs federal law enforcement to review its case files and submit to joint terrorism task forces any materials with a whiff of connection to the ideologies laid out in NSPM-7.

It also identifies laws that could be used by local and state police to target individuals for federal prosecution. Many of these appear to suggest a foundation for targeting protesters.

They include: assaulting, resisting, or impeding federal officers; obstruction during civil disorders; conspiracy to impede or injure officer; obstruction of judicial or agency proceedings; picketing or parading with intent to obstruct the administration of justice; interference with commerce by threats or violence; seditious conspiracy to prevent, hinder, or delay the execution of any law of the United States; and obstruction or disruption in or on federal property.

The statutory definitions of domestic terrorism in the US Code do not have corresponding criminal charging statutes.This has created a practice where a domestic-extremism designation carries enormous surveillance and investigative power, while formal charges must be brought under entirely different statutes. There is a significant range of criminal behavior that meets the definition of domestic terrorism but is prosecuted via nonterrorism charges, meaning that trials may exclude any hint of the investigative resources used to make the case.

This mirrors the logic of NSPM-7. A handful of high-profile cases related to so-called left-wing terrorism have surfaced over the past year – including the trial of the Prairieland nine, the charging of four anti-capitalists in southern California, and the arrest and prosecution of a self-declared antifa member in Portland, Oregon. All three of these cases included either a threat or the carrying out of actual violence tied in some way to a left-wing ideology.

But dozens, even hundreds more cases are likely being assembled that will never disclose how the case came to be: through NSPM-7 and the targeting of left-wing beliefs. This is a major priority for the FBI, which has created a new “NSPM-7 Joint Mission Center,” as Ken Klippenstein reported Sunday.

Despite the whole-of-government approach aimed at curbing dissent, Americans have taken to the streets in droves to protest the administration’s violent, masked federal agents storming into cities from New York to Minneapolis.

In the face of ongoing efforts to silence protesters, whether by gunning them down in cold blood, or by mobilizing the entire surveillance apparatus of the Department of Justice to spy on them, Americans continue to show up in the streets to demonstrate that their constitutional rights are alive and well.

In addition to protesting, there is another grassroots approach that has found success in the past for hitting back at the exact type of federalization of local law enforcement that NSPM-7 encourages: pressing cities and states to opt out of local partnerships with the FBI and fusion centers.

In the past decade, three municipalities have used their city councils to opt out of information sharing agreements with the FBI: Portland, San Francisco, and Oakland all terminated their agreements. The same tactic would be just as effective in compelling local law enforcement and state police to reject NSPM-7-related programming.

In 1965, as the first Marines landed in Da Nang to fight in the Vietnam war, the FBI’s Cointelpro program was swinging into full effect, surveilling and disrupting socialists, civil rights activists, and everyday American citizens who spoke out against US involvement in a conflict that was spiraling out of control.

At the time, the breadth and the scope of the FBI’s targeting of left-wing activists seemed unshakable in its power and breath. Ten years later, its architects were hauled before Senate committees, and forced to tear down the machine they had built in secret.

As a new, deeply unpopular war rages in the Middle East, Trump’s administration is expanding its war on left-wing opposition. Fortunately, thanks to public opposition, Trump has already cast out two of his secret war’s top generals – Pam Bondi and Kristi Noem – and mass discontent will only continue to grow.

Check out more from Zeteo:

items.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] THE VISIONARIES
items.author: Tom Tomorrow
items.content:
Click for larger image.  If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here. 

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning. 

*  *  *

Greetings, readers of the future! I’m writing these words on Thursday, to be queued up for weekend distribution. This is something I always struggle with, given that I usually queue up the newsletter shortly before it goes out, keeping an eye on the news the whole time to make sure you-know-who hasn’t done something that negates the previous week’s work somehow. But I had a curtailed work week due to upcoming weekend travel, which incidentally always screws up my writing process. Even though I generally try to have the cartoon wrapped up by Friday, part of being able to do that is knowing that I have the weekend as a buffer zone in case I run into a mental block or something happens in the news that necessitates a change to the cartoon. It’s pretty neurotic, I’m aware, but this is the brain I’ve got to work with and I do the best I can with it. It makes travel complicated; I haven’t been on a proper vacation since 2023. I’m hoping to switch that up this summer, even if it means running the occasional evergreen that, like this one, may end up feeling completely out of sync with the news cycle, depending on what history’s most special boy Donald J. Trump does on any given week. Of course, with rising travel costs and the general global uncertainty unleashed by history’s most special boy, well. We shall see if these best laid plans gang a-gley.

Anyway I decided to go with an evergreen from my drafts folder this week. Which gives you all that rarest of treats, a cartoon that does not even mention Donald Trump! I’ve had this one mostly ready to go for awhile, though once I pulled it out I had to keep poking and prodding at it, because that’s just how I roll. It took me awhile to decide which tech billionaires to use. Altman and Zuckerberg felt obligatory, but Musk could have been just as easily replaced with Bezos. (Any more than three of them would have crowded the panel too much.) And I try to keep my cartoons internally consistent, so I felt that while I was presenting them as a united front, I also needed to add the line about how they all hate each other — Musk and Altman, in particular, are currently feuding, and of course Musk and Zuckerberg once talked about having an MMA style fight, though Musk backed out of that. I was also aware that Sparky’s rundown of a.i. drawbacks was incomplete, and certain that I would be endlessly reminded of this throughout the week, so I added his line “and that’s just for starters.” A.i. proponents will undoubtedly let me know that ACTUALLY the technology has *many* uses while ignoring the secondary question of the cartoon — at what cost? This is all being shoved down our throats by a handful of billionaires who want to reshape the world in their image and don’t care much about consequences. Are the benefits they claim worth the economic and environmental havoc they generate? Is your chatbot pal worth wrecking the entire world? It reminds me of a novel that I read in high school, by Thomas M. Disch, called The Genocides, about an invasive alien plant species that colonizes Earth and eventually makes it unlivable. I feel like we’re watching the digital version of this happening in real time and I am not a fan. 

ANYWAY! I’m off soon on my whirlwind trip to my old stomping grounds, Iowa City, a make-up trip I promised last January when I had to cancel an appearance there due to the gigantic blizzard. I fly in late Friday and out Sunday morning, so I won’t have as much time there as I would have preferred. I wanted to stay an extra day but the flight schedules for Monday just didn’t work out. Maybe I’ll see/will have seen some of you there! As I say, this is a missive from the distant past of last Thursday. 

Wish list thanks to Michael B., Nova V., and the (probably unintentionally) anonymous gifter who sent the cooking pan (no gift receipt, once again). I’m not proud of this, but when I was first divorced I barely knew how to cook, and I left all the good cookware with my ex. It made sense at the time, our son was still in high school and she was going to be cooking for the two of them. One of her parting gifts on my way out the door was a set of really cheap, terrible cookware, the kind you might buy for a kid going off to college for the first time. Now it’s eight years later and I have become a normal human who cooks pretty regularly, and that stuff is in bad shape — it’s getting beat up, and it’s teflon, so it’s possibly even hazardous to my health at this point — and I’m starting to replace it with, you know, grown-up cookware. I’m 65 years old now, it feels like time! Now if only I could learn to dress like an adult! But you can go a long way with t-shirts and jeans when you are a cartoonist.

Okay, I think that’s it for this week. I appreciate you all, you’re literally the reason I am still able to do this work. Given that I have mostly outlived the altweekly industry that birthed my career, that’s no small thing.

Until next time,

Dan

items.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] WHAT WILL HE SCREW UP TODAY
items.author: Tom Tomorrow
items.content:
This week's TMW!

------------------------------------------------------------

Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=d9698d02ad&e=ed35377427) .

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning.

* * *

Well. Having just had that milestone birthday (celebrated at different points through the weekend with my son and various friends), I think I am now officially a wizened political observer, and friends: I've never seen anything like this last week. The president of the United States posts “open the fuckin’ strait you crazy bastards” and threatens that “a whole civilization will die tonight.” You’d think that even Republicans in Congress would be a little uneasy at the prospect of a deranged dementia case igniting World War III, but I guess you would be mistaken. And until they start to turn on him, removal from office isn't going to happen, though I'm still in the "impeach him anyway" camp, because it's the right thing to do, and it's a matter of political messaging. (Not that Democratic leadership is interested in even raising the issue (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=6da3abbfbd&e=ed35377427) .) Anyway this cartoon is not about the

situation in Iran, exactly, and it’s definitely not meant as a 1:1 parallel. It’s about the overall anxiety, the constant sense of dread that is inescapable under Trump’s presidency. We're trapped on a roller coaster ride, and I hate roller coasters. “A whole civilization will die” is the most extreme example so far, but every morning, I wake up and check the news with a sinking feeling in the pit of my stomach, wondering what the absolute fuck this lunatic has done now. Or what he will do in a few hours. And then what he will do after that.

I myself am very tired of it! When I came up with this shirt (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=e1edf38171&e=ed35377427) thirteen or fourteen years ago, I thought it was just an amusing bit. I did not expect it to become my daily mantra.

I don’t actually enjoy writing cartoon after cartoon about Donald J. Trump, history’s most special boy. Those of you who were kind enough to back the Kickstarter, and have copies of OUR LONG NATIONAL NIGHTMARE (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=73bc1606bb&e=ed35377427) , can go back and look through — I had a much wider range of topics when Biden was president. Frankly, Biden rarely made appearances in the strip. I was okay with that. Though thinking back on it, that “wider range of topics” frequently included the possibility that Trump might regain power, and what a horrific prospect that was. And here we are!

And we’re only a year and change in. The only bright spot on the near horizon is the possibility of a blue wave in November, and while I am hoping for that as fervently as any of you, November feels like a long time away right now. A lot can happen between now and then, including the death of whole civilizations. I just don’t think the former host of Celebrity Apprentice should have that power.

NOTE TO IOWANS: my event in Iowa City is on Saturday (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=2d6291cecf&e=ed35377427) , April 18 from 3-5 p.m. at Merge. It’s a panel with four other cartoonists, moderated by Kembrew McLeod. There will apparently be pizza! And even a limited number of copies of OLNN for sale, making this possibly the only opportunity for an in-person booksigning. And if you already have the book, or any of the others, feel feel to bring them, I’ll sign whatever you’ve got! I mean, within reason.

And on a completely different note, this is George. He is 28 years old and lives in the basement of an UWS pet store, down with the aquariums. I used to visit him more often when I had a fish tank, several years ago, and was strolling by on a pleasant afternoon and decided to drop in and see if he was still around. Happily, he is!

And finally, one for the "graphic design is my passion" file.

Wish list thanks this week to Karen C. And that's a wrap on another exhausting week.

Until next time,

Dan

items.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] THE INCREDIBLE SHRINKING TRUMP
items.author: Tom Tomorrow
items.content:
This week's TMW!

------------------------------------------------------------

Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=16069e7db0&e=ed35377427) .

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning.

* * *

OK, this is admittedly a weird one. I was sitting at my desk trying to write a cartoon about How Donald Trump Fucks Up Everything, but it just wasn’t flowing, and felt flat and uninspired, and I just wanted to have some fun. I had a random thought about a doing a 50 Foot Woman bit, with Trump growing oversized and out of control, but then I got to thinking that actually it’s the opposite, we’re watching him grow diminished in real time, with approval ratings at historic lows even compared to his first term. Now, I have only the vaguest memory of The Incredible Shrinking Man, which I guess I probably saw as a kid at some point, so I took a quick look at the summary, and saw that the character starts shrinking after being exposed to a “mysterious cloud of gas.” I started turning that over in my head, and it occurred to me that Elon Musk is constantly being fined for dumping toxic waste from his various enterprises, so I thought that would be an appropriate way to start the ball rolling.

Panel two sets up the premise (obvious from the title of the strip of course). The third panel was a no-brainer — faced with a medical mystery like this, who does Trump have on hand to call? The experts he has put in charge of the nation’s health care system, obviously! The fourth and fifth panels just came to me unbidden — Trump in front of a giant phone, texting with both hands (note the bruise), characteristically denying that there are any problems whatsoever, and then getting swept off the desk by an errant feather duster. (Trump actually did misspell “strait” in a post, which put me in an awkward position — if I spelled it correctly, readers would be reminding me of the misspelling all week, but if I misspelled it, other readers would think the mistake was mine. I decided to thread the needle by putting the word in Trumpian quotes.) Then the question was, how to finish up? I could have gone with his ultimate fate — and if there were a White House cat, that would have clearly been the

way to end it, per the classic movie still of the tiny guy being chased by a giant cat, but of course everyone knows Trump hates pets, so that wasn’t gonna work. And then my mind wandered back to the original 50 Foot Woman riff, and I decided to close with that, using Miller to give the cartoon a bit of an ominous ending — even though we will eventually be done with Trump, the fascist, authoritarian movement he has empowered will still be with us. It also seemed appropriate to portray Miller as a vengeful lunatic with far too much power.

There are a lot of other panels I could have included in this, with so many potential scenarios, such as the reaction of right wing media to the shrinking president. I wrote one panel that didn’t make it in with Jesse Watters saying, “We must stand behind our tiny president — but be careful not to step ON him!” Which is a pretty good line, but there just wasn’t quite enough room.

Last week’s cartoon, with shirtless RFK eating fried rats, Trump in Sherlockian mode, “Drinky Guy” Hegseth and Bellhop Vance, still somehow managed to inspire a couple of “this isn’t satire, it’s just what’s really happening” comments, which long time readers may remember is my absolute least-favorite genre of feedback, as it both cheapens the effort I put into these things and absolutely misunderstands the nature of satire. If someone looks at *this* week’s cartoon and is inspired to post that comment, I think my head may literally explode. (Fortunately I’m starting up on Medicare on April 1!)

And finally, a few quick shots from Saturday's No Kings march, starting off with Padma Lakshi, Deniro and Sharpton at the front of the march:

And this was fun -- people send me pics all the time but I rarely run across my own work in the wild at these things. The Trump face on the sign is a mask IDW gave out at New York Comic Con in 2016, before he'd even been elected. Because it was such an early caricature, I had to look at it twice to be certain it was actually my own work!

Okay. Wish list thanks this week to David B., Barbara H., and (as often happens) an unintentionally anonymous gifter. Speaking of, several readers have alerted me to the changes in Amazon’s policy which might make it possible for my home address to be revealed, which of course I’m cautious about. But having dug into it a bit, it seems that this only applies to third-party sellers, so I’ll just be avoiding those.

And, one more reminder -- we currently have +/- 70 unsigned copies of OLNN remaining, and about 30 signed. If you want one, or want another one to give to someone, act quickly, (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=90ed3d1ae3&e=ed35377427) because we are close to clearing these things out.

Until next week!

items.title: [SPARKY'S-LIST] DETECTIVE-IN-CHIEF
items.author: Tom Tomorrow
items.content:
This week's TMW!

------------------------------------------------------------

Click for larger image. If you are asked for a password, the login is "sparky" and the pass is "blinky"

READ THIS: if you need to change your credit card info, you can request a link to your account automatically, here (https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=1cf669bb1e&e=ed35377427) .

REMINDER: we're on the honor system here, but PLEASE do not forward this newsletter! And please don't share the cartoon on social media until it goes live on Monday morning.

* * *

My challenge each week, or at least most weeks, is figuring out how to encapsulate the ongoing, omnidirectional insanity and chaos of this administration. RFK Jr. doesn’t have anything to do with the main theme of this cartoon, but I wanted to include him as a reminder that there’s always something else going on at the same time, and it’s exhausting. As Trump and Hegseth celebrate Schroedinger’s war, which we have already won and yet are continuing to escalate, RFK is still working to tank the nation’s health infrastructure, Stephen Miller is still plotting the expulsion of everyone he hates, apparently Tom Homan is spending the day planning to deploy ICE to airports tomorrow, etc., etc. Also RFK released a weird a.i. video last week showing himself shirtless, fighting a (computelr generated) guy in a Twinkie suit in a wrestling ring, and I just thought it would be funny if shirtless RFK became a recurrent theme, like bellhop Vance. Walz had it exactly right in 2024 — these people are just

*weird*.

Newt Gingrich really did suggest nuking a new opening as an alternative to the Strait of Hormuz. And I wanted to at least gesture toward the fact that Vance is walking a tightrope, forced to support his degenerate master’s pointless war, but trying to signal that he’s not fully on board, because he knows full well that this is all going to go sideways in a big hurry and he still dreams of being president some day. And of course, Hegseth did say, out loud, that he can’t wait until Ellison takes over CNN so news coverage will be more favorable. Which is exactly how Putin handled the media in the early aughts; the second wave of oligarchs were Putin allies, and they took over Russian news media to ensure favorable coverage.

I set this one on the White House lawn just for a change of pace, visually, from all the Oval Office cartoons I do.

It is a strange moment, in which we stand on the brink of an abyss. I mean, yes, it feels like we’ve been standing on the brink of an abyss for quite a long time now, but now we’re facing a global oil shock, a global economic meltdown, even global famine as fertilizer supplies have been interrupted by the Hormuz closure. (I owe some taxes this year, due to the Kickstarter, but at least I can rest secure in the knowledge that my money will be spent on a pointless war that’s going to make everything more expensive!) And those are just the ancillary complications of this idiotic war of choice, which could absolutely escalate at a moment’s notice. Meanwhile, Donald Trump obsesses over his ballroom, and having the mint produce a gold coin with his face on it. And he also wants his birthday made into a federal holiday, his face on Mount Rushmore (that one’s old news, but apparently still an active obsession), and a “Trump-shaped monument built in our nation’s capital.

(https://thismodernworld.us5.list-manage.com/track/click?u=8c0b845e697a5327910af76a9&id=c201ad6f94&e=ed35377427) ” He may set some sort of record for the inverse relationship between his tanking approval and the number of monuments to himself he leaves behind. It all reminds me of this cartoon from 2017:

I’ve got some wish list thanks that will need to wait until next week. Also, it seems that it might be time to wind down the wish list, as Amazon has made some changes which could potentially expose my home address. I’m keeping an eye on that.

Finally, since it has been awhile, here's a bonus picture of the indolent gentleman of leisure with whom I share my apartment.

Until next week!

Dan

items.title: Judicial Notice (04.19.26): Please Stop!
items.author: David Lat
items.content:
This week’s Judicial Notice is sponsored by

Burford Capital helps companies and law firms unlock the value of their legal assets. With a portfolio of over $7 billion and listings on the NYSE and LSE, Burford provides capital to finance high-value commercial litigation and arbitration—without adding cost or risk or giving up control. Clients include Fortune 500 companies and Am Law 100 firms, who turn to Burford to pursue strong claims, manage legal costs and accelerate recoveries. Learn more at burfordcapital.com.

Last Wednesday, I flew up to Ithaca to speak about Trump v. Biglaw to the Cornell Federalist Society. I didn’t know they had invited the controversial conservative lawyer John Eastman to address them on Thursday—and on Wednesday night, just hours after his disbarment was upheld by the California Supreme Court, Eastman joined me and some of the FedSoc officers for dinner.

I was nervous about the prospect of dining with Dr. Eastman, since I have criticized him somewhat harshly in the past. But we wound up having a perfectly pleasant dinner, despite our differences on everything from January 6 to birthright citizenship.

While I understand that many Eastman critics would be unwilling to break bread with him, I believe it’s important to spend time with people we disagree with. I also used the occasion to do some reporting, asking him about his disbarment. He told me he plans to appeal—and he believes that the U.S. Supreme Court’s recent ruling in Chiles v. Salazar, aka the conversion-therapy case, supports his claim that his election-related legal work for Donald Trump was a proper exercise of his free-speech rights as a lawyer. As Justice Neil Gorsuch wrote in Chiles, “the First Amendment’s protections extend to licensed professionals much as they do to everyone else.”

A housekeeping note (based on reader correspondence I’ve recently received): if you need to update the email address where you receive OJ, simply follow these instructions (or email support@substack.com with the old and new addresses, and they’ll fix it). If you subscribe, I want to make sure you’re getting your full value.

Now, on to the news.

Lawyers of the Week: Jeffrey Kessler, Eva Cole, Jen Parsigian, Josh Hafenbrack, Johanna Hudgens, Jonathan Hatch, Adam Gitlin, and the entire trial team in United States v. Live Nation.

Not many Biglaw partners get profiled and interviewed by Rolling Stone. But not many partners qualify as legal “rock star[s],” to use the sobriquet that publication bestowed upon Jeffrey Kessler, co-executive chairman of Winston & Strawn, and—when he’s not busy running that 1,000-lawyer firm—a top trial lawyer, focusing on antitrust as a practice area and sports and entertainment as an industry.

Given his expertise in trying cases, antitrust, and sports and entertainment, Kessler was a perfect choice to pick up the plaintiffs’ case in United States v. Live Nation, the sprawling, complex, years-long antitrust case against the world’s largest live-entertainment company (and owner of Ticketmaster, the concert giant that merged with Live Nation in 2010). Why did Kessler and Winston need to step in? As I explained last month, the U.S. Department of Justice was leading the charge in this case—but after the Trump administration and six states reached a surprise settlement with Live Nation in early March, the 33 other states (and D.C.), which wanted to continue pursuing Live Nation, needed a new quarterback for the trial team.

Kessler had only eight days to get up to speed with the case, which was already being tried before a jury and Judge Arun Subramanian (S.D.N.Y.). Kessler and his colleagues pulled off that feat, then went on to score an across-the-board win for the plaintiffs in this landmark antitrust litigation. As you can see from the lengthy verdict form, the jury found liability on all claims—a combination of federal and state antitrust, unfair trade practices, and consumer protection laws. (In a statement, Live Nation emphasized that the jury’s verdict “is not the last word,” and the company will be disputing both liability and damages in post-trial motions and on appeal.)

In an interview with Ross Todd of Am Law Litigation Daily, which named him Litigator of the Week, Kessler shared the victory with the members of his “trial SWAT team.” That group included Eva Cole, Jen Parsigian, Josh Hafenbrack, and Johanna Hudgens, from Winston; Jonathan Hatch and Adam Gitlin, from the New York and D.C. AG’s offices, respectively; and, in Kessler’s words, “a group of hard-working lawyers from the 34 states that remained in the case.”

In light of her role in this epic, plaintiff-side win, I’m looking forward to speaking with Eva Cole on May 6. I’m moderating a panel about Biglaw’s move into plaintiff-side litigation, featuring Cole, Paul Hastings partner Ryan Phair, and Burford managing director Evan Meyerson. For details and to RSVP, head over to the event page.

Other lawyers in the news:

Speaking of top trial lawyers, Hailey Fuchs penned a fun profile of David Oscar Markus for Politico. His highest-profile client right now isn’t as sympathetic as states going after Ticketmaster: Markus represents Ghislaine Maxwell, the convicted sex offender and ex-paramour of Jeffrey Epstein. (If you enjoy Fuchs’s piece, give a listen to my podcast interview of Markus.)

Speaking of lawyers who great results for clients, kudos to Roberta Kaplan and her team at Kaplan Martin—including Brandon Trice, Max Crema, Avita Anand, Thomas Lloyd, and Olivia Berci—for getting the Trump administration to release $60 million in withheld funding for Manhattan’s Second Avenue subway extension. (As a straphanger myself, I’m personally thankful for this win.)

Maria Medetis Long (S.D. Fla.), an experienced career prosecutor, is no longer part of the investigation into former CIA director turned Trump critic John Brennan. According to CNN, which first reported on her departure, she was removed by the DOJ; according to The Times (gift link), she withdrew over concerns about the viability of the potential case against Brennan. The investigation into Brennan—which reportedly focuses on allegations that he made willfully false statements about the DOJ investigation of Russian interference in the 2016 election, during a 2023 congressional deposition—will now be led by former U.S. attorney Joseph diGenova, an outspoken critic of the Russia probe.

In memoriam:

Ishmael Jaffree—the plaintiff in the famous school-prayer case of Wallace v. Jaffree, as well as a lawyer himself—passed away at 80. He actually died in 2024, but his passing didn’t become public until last week, when it was reported by The New York Times (gift link, via SCOTUStoday).

Former Virginia lieutenant governor Justin Fairfax killed his wife, Cerina — and then himself—in their home on Thursday morning, as reported by The Washington Post (gift link). A graduate of Columbia Law, former federal law clerk and prosecutor (E.D. Va.), and Biglaw alum, Fairfax was a rising star of the Democratic Party, until his career was derailed by sexual-assault allegations. He and Cerina, a dentist who was devoted to her patients, were divorcing.

May they rest in peace.

Judge of the Week: Judge Richard Leon!

Last weekend, the D.C. Circuit remanded the legal challenge to Trump’s White House ballroom project to Judge Richard Leon (D.D.C.), who had ordered a halt to the controversial construction project. The panel majority, consisting of Judges Patricia Millett and Bradley Garcia, directed Judge Leon to take another look and clarify certain aspects of his ruling. Judge Neomi Rao dissented; she would have stayed the preliminary injunction entirely, allowing the construction to proceed.

On Thursday, Judge Leon issued a new ruling, which Reuters described as “peppered with his trademark exclamation marks and sarcasm.” He once again ordered a halt to construction, but stayed his order to allow time for appeal. Donald Trump, a fellow devotee of the exclamation point, promptly took to Truth Social to trash Judge Leon:

The White House doesn’t have a Ballroom (No Taxpayer Money!), which Presidents have desperately wanted and desired for over 150 years, but a Trump Hating, Washington, D.C. District Court Judge, a man who has gone out of his way to undermine National Security, and to make sure that this Great Gift to America gets delayed, or doesn’t get built, is attempting to prevent future Presidents and World Leaders from having a safe and secure large scale Meeting Place, or Ballroom….

This highly political Judge, and his illegal overreach, is out of control, and costing our Nation greatly. This is a mockery to our Court System! The Ballroom is deeply important to our National Security, and no Judge can be allowed to stop this Historic and Militarily Imperative Project. Thank you for your attention to this matter!

The D.C. Circuit gave its attention to this matter. In a two-page order, the panel expedited the appeal, with oral argument set for June 5, and administratively stayed Judge Leon’s preliminary injunction—i.e., construction can continue for now.

This isn’t Judge Leon’s first entanglement with the Trump administration. Last year, he ruled against the administration in WilmerHale’s challenge to the executive order against it, as well as the Pentagon’s attempt to reduce Senator Mark Kelly’s retired military rank and pension pay (after Kelly urged troops to reject unlawful orders).

As noted by Reuters, Judge Leon, 76, has “conservative bona fides.” Prior to his 2002 appointment by George W. Bush, he worked for Republicans on Capitol Hill and at the DOJ during the Reagan administration. He also counseled now-Justice Clarence Thomas, a college classmate, during his contentious confirmation—and I can’t help wondering what Judge Leon thinks of his old friend’s votes and opinions in Trump-related cases. (As for Judge Leon’s own writings, I’m with Advisory Opinions: “No more exclamation points in judicial opinions!!!”)

In other news about judges and the judiciary (with my usual thanks to Howard Bashman of How Appealing, who was quite busy this weekend):

As reported by Fox News and confirmed by Jan Crawford of CBS News, Justice Samuel Alito is not retiring from the Supreme Court this year (nor is Justice Clarence Thomas, but we already knew that). I’m relieved by the reported correctness of my predictions—and by not having to cover a SCOTUS retirement and confirmation in this already exhausting year, barring something unforeseen. (But if we do have a surprise vacancy, Donald Trump said he’s ready, with a list of potential candidates already prepared.)

Speaking of the Court, Jodi Kantor and Adam Liptak published a fascinating article in The New York Times (gift link), based on leaked memos (gift link), about a 2016 case that they and some legal scholars view as “the birth of the modern shadow docket.” (But see, e.g., this thread by Sarah Isgur—author of Last Branch Standing, and my most recent

To her credit, Justice Sonia Sotomayor apologized for what she described in a statement as “inappropriate” remarks about a colleague—unnamed in the statement, but known to be Justice Brett Kavanaugh. (I’ve updated my stories discussing her comments, last week’s Judicial Notice and my Isgur interview.)

To his credit, Chief Justice John Roberts, while at the University of Virginia to receive an award, made surprise visits to two UVA Law classes—to the disbelief of students, one of whom “thought it was a prank at first.”

Congratulations to Judges J. Paul Oetken (S.D.N.Y.) and Jesse Furman (S.D.N.Y.)—not on their track records as Supreme Court feeder judges (although they are), but on being named the #1 and #2 favorite federal judges of New York litigators. Kudos also to Chief Judge Laura Swain (S.D.N.Y.) and Judge Frederic Block (E.D.N.Y.), who took third and fourth place in the poll by Bloomberg Law.

In nominations news, the Senate confirmed two state-court judges to district-court seats, Judges John Shepherd (W.D. Ark.) and Chris Wolfe (W.D. Tex.). Fun fact: John Shepherd is the son of Eighth Circuit Judge Bobby Shepherd, one of around 18 Republican-appointed circuit judges who’s eligible to take senior status but hasn’t.

Job of the Week: an opportunity for a corporate M&A associate in Chicago.

Lateral Link is assisting a repeat client, a top-ranked Am Law 25 firm, in its search for a corporate M&A associate with 2–6 years of experience. The role involves advising public and private companies, as well as private equity sponsors and their portfolio companies, on a broad range of transactions—M&A, stock and asset purchases, LBOs, platform and add-on acquisitions, joint ventures, and growth equity investments. Associates receive hands-on deal experience, meaningful responsibility, and early client interaction, with strong long-term partnership and in-house opportunities. Interested and qualified candidates should submit their résumé here or email Jesse Hyde at jhyde@laterallink.com.

Ruling of the Week: In re: Donald J. Trump .

We’re getting close to high season for the U.S. Supreme Court, but we’re not there yet. If you joined me for the SCOTUSblog live blog on Friday, you were probably disappointed by the single, unanimous ruling: Chevron USA Inc. v. Plaquemines Parish, an opinion by Justice Thomas about the

But less than a mile away, the D.C. Circuit was busy. In addition to handling the ballroom litigation discussed above, they issued 122 pages of opinions in In re: Donald J. Trump—aka the legal battle over Chief Judge

It appears the inquiry might have come to an end. On Tuesday, a divided panel of the D.C. Circuit ordered a stop to the contempt proceedings—or, if you want to be more technical about it, the majority issued a writ of mandamus “to confine the district court to its lawful jurisdiction and terminate these criminal contempt proceedings.” Here’s a CliffsNotes-style summary of the long and detailed opinions:

Judge Neomi Rao, joined by Judge Justin Walker: “Judge Boasberg, please stop trying to make ‘contempt’ happen. It’s not going to happen.” Or to quote from the opinion, the order “lacks the clarity to support criminal contempt”—i.e., its wording wasn’t sufficiently specific to establish a clear violation by the DOJ—and the contemplated contempt inquiry “improperly threaten[s] an open-ended, freewheeling inquiry into Executive Branch decision-making on matters of national security that implicate ongoing military and diplomatic initiatives.”

Judge Walker, concurring: based on a close reading of the hearing transcript and subsequent written order, “the Government did not violate the oral order,” and once the oral order was superseded by the written order, “the Government did not violate the written order.” No violation, no contempt.

Judge J. Michelle Childs, dissenting (in 80 pages or so, almost twice as long as the other opinions combined): “Contempt of court is a public offense, and the fate of our democratic republic will depend on whether we treat it as such…. It is because of the utmost respect I have for the trial court and the gift of our system of governance that I believe that mandamus should not be used to question a trial court’s inherent authority to protect its courtroom and the rule of law.”

Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche celebrated the ruling on X, tweeting that it “should finally end Judge Boasberg’s year-long campaign against the hardworking Department attorneys doing their jobs fighting illegal immigration.” But I’m not so sure of that. Lee Gelernt of the ACLU, which has been representing the Venezuelan detainees who were sent to El Salvador, told The Times that he plans to seek rehearing en banc—and it might very well get granted.

Last November, at an earlier stage in the litigation, the D.C. Circuit declined to go en banc by a vote of 8-3. But three Democratic appointees who cast the deciding votes—Judges Nina Pillard, Robert Wilkins, and Brad Garcia—didn’t vote for rehearing because, in a nutshell, the court’s prior ruling left open the possibility of contempt proceedings. This latest ruling shuts the door—so I could see the court going en banc this time around, especially given Judge Childs’s forceful and impassioned dissent.

Other noteworthy decisions and dispositions:

Doe v. Blanche. I told you the D.C. Circuit was busy. In an opinion by Judge Pillard, the majority vacated injunctions prohibiting the transfer of 17 incarcerated transgender women to men’s facilities, based on Eighth Amendment claims—but sent the case back to Judge

Beckwith v. Frey. From John Ross of

Baird v. Bonta. In other gun-related legal news, the Ninth Circuit voted to rehear the

Detwiler v. Mid-Columbia Medical Center. Again from Short Circuit: “the Ninth Circuit will not reconsider its decision that an objection to COVID-19 testing was not sufficiently connected to religious doctrine to state a claim under Title VII. Fans of dissentals will be chuffed.” (Judges

Trump v. Dow Jones & Company, Inc. Judge

Litigation of the Week: United States v. Rhodes .

On Tuesday, U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro (D.D.C.), acting on behalf of the Trump administration, asked the D.C. Circuit to vacate the convictions of 12 defendants, members of the Proud Boys and the Oath Keepers, who were involved in the January 6 attack on the Capitol. Donald Trump did not include these individuals, convicted of seditious conspiracy, in his pardon of January 6 offenders—but he did commute their sentences, freeing them from federal prison. (The defendant most well-known to OJ readers is Stewart Rhodes, a Yale Law School graduate and lawyer by training, who received an 18-year prison sentence for seditious conspiracy.)

Not content with that largesse, the Proud Boys and Oath Keepers appealed their convictions. Instead of urging the D.C. Circuit to affirm, the Trump administration asked the court to vacate—and said that when the cases return to the district court, the government will move to dismiss all underlying charges. If judges grant the government’s motions, the defendants’ records in these cases will be cleared.

Why is the administration going down this path? Per The Washington Post (gift link), “Pirro gave no details explaining her action, saying only that ‘the United States has determined in its prosecutorial discretion that dismissal of this criminal case is in the interests of justice.’” But according to The New York Times (gift link), defending the convictions “would likely have required administration officials to assert that the far-right groups were acting on behalf of Mr. Trump on January 6”—not a great look.

Former Metropolitan Police Officer Michael Fanone, who had a heart attack after a rioter shocked him with a stun gun, told NPR he was disappointed but not surprised by the latest turn of events. “I would remind Americans that these were traitors to this country,” he said. “They planned, incited and carried out an insurrection.”

In other litigation news:

According to the Trump administration’s latest filings in the tariff-refund litigation in the Court of International Trade, its online platform for processing refunds is ready to go—and will go live tomorrow (Monday). Fingers crossed.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX agreed to settle its litigation against the California Coastal Commission, in which SpaceX alleged that the Commission as going after SpaceX’s rocket launches in retaliation against Musk’s political views. (The settlement terms are not yet known.)

Speaking of settlements, the Trump administration negotiated a deal with the American Library Association and a union of cultural workers, in which the administration will stop trying to defund or otherwise eliminate the Institute of Museum and Library Services, a federal agency.

Deal of the Week: Amazon’s $11.6 billion acquisition of Globalstar.

Amazon.com agreed to purchase Globalstar Inc., the satellite telecommunications company, for around $11.6 billion, in an effort to catch up to Elon Musk’s Starlink. The law firms are Paul Weiss for Amazon; Skadden Arps for Globalstar; Fried Frank for Evercore, financial adviser to Globalstar’s special committee; and Sullivan & Cromwell for Goldman Sachs, financial adviser to Amazon.

In other deal news:

American Airlines said “thanks, but no thanks” to the idea of merging with United Airlines, an idea reportedly floated by United CEO Scott Kirby.

Sazerac, which produces Buffalo Trace bourbon, offered to buy Brown-Forman, the parent company of Jack Daniel's, for around $15 billion, per the WSJ (gift link).

In antitrust litigation brought by states and a private party (but not the feds), Chief Judge Troy Nunley (E.D. Cal.) issued an injunction ordering the television giant Nexstar to permit Tegna—another TV station owner, which Nexstar acquired for $6.2 billion—to let Tegna continue operating separately. In a statement, Nexstar said that the eggs are already scrambled: the parties closed their merger a month ago, after getting approval from the FCC and the DOJ.

Law Firm of the Week: DLA Piper.

On Monday, after a one-week trial before Judge Analisa Torres (S.D.N.Y.), a jury in Manhattan federal court found that DLA Piper did not discriminate against former IP associate Anisha Mehta, as reported by Reuters and Bloomberg Law. Represented by Wigdor, Mehta alleged that she was fired in 2022 because she requested parental leave. But DLA Piper—represented by some of its own lawyers, as well as litigators from Gibson Dunn—claimed that Mehta was fired because of poor performance.

In his opening statement, DLA Piper partner Brett Ingerman highlighted mistakes allegedly made by Mehta—such as filing a trademark infringement action in Singapore, when it was supposed to have been filed in Switzerland—and the fact that Mehta was fired by a partner who herself took parental leave twice during her time as an associate. After the verdict, Ingerman told Law.com, “I was proud to represent the law firm I’ve called home for the last 32 years. DLA Piper and its lawyers are committed to fostering an environment that promotes the family journey.”

Move of the Week: the launch of Liu Shur Kravis.

The trend of Biglaw partners leaving for boutiques continues. The latest lawyers to leave are Jessie Liu, the former U.S. attorney for D.C. who departed from Skadden Arps, and former federal prosecutor Jonathan Kravis, who moved from Munger Tolles & Olson. Joined by Justin Shur—an alum of the Manhattan District Attorney’s Office and Main Justice who was already at a leading boutique, MoloLamken—they’ve launched Liu Shur Kravis, as reported by Reuters and Law.com.

Many D.C. boutiques have an ideological bent—e.g., Cooper & Kirk and Consovoy McCarthy on the right, and Gupta Wessler and Hecker Fink on the left—but LSK is impressively bipartisan. The founders have high-level government service in both Republican and Democratic administrations: Liu was the Senate-confirmed U.S. attorney for D.C. in the first Trump administration, while Kravis worked in the White House Counsel’s Office in the Obama administration. (I plan to include Jonathan Kravis in my forthcoming guide to pronouncing the tougher names of certain lawyers and judges; the first syllable of “Kravis” rhymes with “brave,” not “have.”)

LSK’s three Chambers-ranked partners have extensive experience handling high-stakes, headline-making matters. Liu and Kravis worked together on the prosecution of Trump ally Roger Stone; Shur counseled Kevin ⁠Clinesmith, a former FBI lawyer who was investigated by the late Robert Mueller; and Kravis recently represented Tom Goldstein (but I wouldn’t blame Kravis for the outcome, given the hand he was dealt). If I found myself in deep doo-doo in D.C.—or, really, anywhere in the country—LSK would be on my list of law firms to consider.

Other notable moves:

Also in the Washington white-collar space, Dechert hired Tara Lee, Scott Lerner, and Melissa Taylormoore, who joined from White & Case.

Once again, Cravath, Wachtell Lipton, and Skadden topped the Vault prestige rankings—but in today’s lateral market, they’re not impervious to poaching:

Sidley Austin welcomed capital-markets partner Ellen Park from Cravath—the tenth partner to leave the storied firm in 2026 (although note that she made partner only this year, and Cravath already edited her out of the new-partner announcement—but you can see her name on the Google cache).

Kirkland & Ellis is luring Joshua Feltman away from Wachtell, where he chaired the restructuring and finance department.

Launching in Los Angeles, Davis Polk plans to pick up litigator Jason Russell from Skadden, whose L.A. office he leads.

Lowenstein Sandler added a four-lawyer bankruptcy team from Polsinelli, where partner Shanti Mulpuru Katona served as co-chair of the practice.

Thanks for joining me for JN—and once again, for those of you in the New York City metropolitan area, I hope to see some of you on May 6 for my breakfast event with Burford Capital. Have a wonderful week.

This post is only for paid subscribers of Original Jurisdiction . As always, thanks for your support!
